ITEM 1.  BUSINESS
In this report, the terms "The Coca-Cola Company," "Company," "we," "us" and "our" mean The Coca-Cola Company and all entities included in our consolidated financial statements.
General
The Coca-Cola Company is a total beverage company, and beverage products bearing our trademarks, sold in the United States since 1886, are now sold in more than 200 countries and territories. We own or license and market numerous beverage brands, which we group into the following categories: Trademark Coca-Cola; sparkling flavors; water, sports, coffee and tea; juice, value-added dairy and plant-based beverages; and emerging beverages. We own and market five of the world's top six nonalcoholic sparkling soft drink brands: Coca-Cola, Sprite, Fanta, Coca-Cola Zero Sugar and Diet Coke/Coca-Cola Light.
We make our branded beverage products available to consumers throughout the world through our network of independent bottling partners, distributors, wholesalers and retailers as well as our consolidated bottling and distribution operations. Beverages bearing trademarks owned by or licensed to the Company account for 2.2 billion of the estimated 64 billion servings of all beverages consumed worldwide every day.
We believe our success depends on our ability to connect with consumers by providing them with a wide variety of beverage options to meet their desires, needs and lifestyles. Our success further depends on the ability of our people to execute effectively, every day.
We are guided by our purpose, which is to refresh the world and make a difference. Our vision for growth has three connected pillars:
- Loved Brands. We craft meaningful brands and a choice of drinks that people love and enjoy and that refresh them in body and spirit.
- Done Sustainably. We grow our business in ways that achieve positive change in the world and build a more sustainable future for our planet.
- For A Better Shared Future. We invest to improve people's lives, from our employees to all those who touch our business system, to our investors, to the broad communities we call home.
The Coca-Cola Company was incorporated in September 1919 under the laws of the State of Delaware and succeeded to the business of a Georgia corpor
…[truncated 19780 chars]…
djusted periodically by the Company, up to a maximum indexed ceiling price, and is adjusted quarterly based upon changes in certain sugar or sweetener prices, as applicable. The U.S. unit case volume prepared, packaged, sold and distributed under these legacy bottler's agreements is not material.
Under the terms of the bottler's agreements, bottlers in the United States generally are not authorized to manufacture fountain syrups. Rather, the Company manufactures and sells fountain syrups to authorized fountain wholesalers (including certain authorized bottlers) and some fountain retailers. These wholesalers in turn sell the syrups, or deliver them on our behalf, to restaurants and other retailers.
6
Promotional and Marketing Programs
In addition to conducting our own independent advertising and marketing activities, we may provide promotional and marketing support and/or funds to our bottlers. In most cases, we do this on a discretionary basis under the terms of commitment letters or agreements, even though we are not obligated to do so under the terms of the bottler's agreements between our Company and the bottlers. Also, on a discretionary basis in most cases, our Company may develop and introduce new products, packages and equipment to assist the bottlers. Likewise, in many instances, we provide promotional and marketing support and/or funds and/or dispensing equipment and repair services to fountain and bottle/can retailers, typically pursuant to marketing agreements. The aggregate amount provided by our Company to bottlers, resellers and other customers  of our Company's products, principally for participation in promotional and marketing programs, was $4.8 billion in 2022.
Investments in Bottling Operations
Most of our branded beverage products are prepared, packaged, distributed and sold by independent bottling partners. However, from time to time we acquire or take control of a bottling operation, often in underperforming markets where we believe we can use our resources and expertise to improve performance. Owning a bottling operation enables us to compensate for limited local resources; help focus the bottler's sales and marketing programs; assist in the development of the bottler's business and information systems; and establish an appropriate capital structure for the bottler. In line with our long-term bottling strategy, we may periodically consider options for divesting or reducing our ownership interest in a consolidated bottling operation, typically by selling all or a portion of our interest in the bottling operation to an independent bottler to improve Coca-Cola system efficiency. When we sell a consolidated bottling operation to an independent bottling partner in which we have an equity method investment, our Company continues to participate in the bottler's results of operations through our share of the equity method investee's earnings or losses.
In addition, from time to time we make equity investments representing noncontrolling interests in certain bottling operations with the intention of maximizing the strength and efficiency of the Coca-Cola system's production, marketing, sales and distribution capabilities around the world by providing expertise and resources to strengthen those businesses. These investments are intended to result in increases in unit case volume, net operating revenues and profits at the bottler level, which in turn generate increased sales for our Company's concentrate operations. When our equity investment provides us with the ability to exercise significant influence over the investee bottler's operating and financial policies, we account for the investment under the equity method.
Seasonality
Sales of our ready-to-drink beverages are somewhat seasonal, with the second and third calendar quarters historically accounting for the highest sales volumes. The volume of sales in the beverage business may be affected by weather conditions.
Competition
The commercial beverage industry is highly competitive and consists of numerous companies, ranging from small or emerging to very large and well established. These include companies that, like our Company, compete globally in multiple geographic areas, as well as businesses that are primarily regional or local in operation. Competitive products include numerous nonalcoholic sparkling soft drinks; water products, including flavored and enhanced waters; juices, juice drinks and nectars; dilutables (including syrups and powders); coffees; teas; energy drinks; sports drinks; milk and other dairy-based drinks; plant-based beverages; functional beverages, including vitamin-based products and relaxation beverages; and various other nonalcoholic beverages. These competitive products are sold to consumers in both ready-to-drink and non-ready-to-drink form. The Company has directly entered the alcohol beverage category in numerous markets outside the United States. In the United States, the Company has authorized alcohol-licensed third parties to use certain of our trademarks and related intellectual property on alcohol beverages that contain Company beverage bases. Competitive products include all flavored alcohol beverages containing various alcohol bases. In many of the countries in which we do business, PepsiCo, Inc. is a primary competitor. Other significant competitors include, but are not limited to, Nestlé S.A., Keurig Dr Pepper Inc., Danone S.A., Suntory Beverage & Food Limited, Unilever, AB InBev, Kirin Holdings, Heineken N.V., Diageo and Red Bull GmbH. We also compete against numerous regional and local companies and, increasingly, against smaller companies that are developing microbrands and selling them directly to consumers through e-commerce retailers and other e-commerce platforms. In addition, in some markets, we compete against retailers that have developed their own store or private-label beverage brands.
Competitive factors impacting our business include, but are not limited to, pricing, advertising, sales promotion programs,     in-store displays and point-of-sale marketing, digital marketing, product and ingredient innovation, increased efficiency in production techniques, the introduction of new packaging as well as new vending and dispensing equipment, contracting with marketing assets (theaters, sports arenas, universities, etc.), and brand and trademark development and protection.
Our competitive strengths include leading brands with high levels of consumer recognition and loyalty; a worldwide network of bottlers and distributors of Company products; sophisticated marketing capabilities; and a talented group of dedicated employees. Our competitive challenges include strong competitors in all geographic regions; in many countries, a concentrated
7
retail sector with powerful buyers able to freely choose among Company products, products of competitive beverage suppliers and individual retailers' own store or private-label beverage brands; new industry entrants; and dramatic shifts in consumer shopping methods and patterns due to a rapidly evolving digital landscape.
Raw Materials
We and our bottling partners use various ingredients in our business, including high fructose corn syrup ("HFCS"), sucrose, aspartame, acesulfame potassium, sucralose, saccharin, cyclamate, steviol glycosides, ascorbic acid, citric acid, phosphoric acid, caffeine and caramel color; other raw materials such as coffee, orange and other fruit juice and juice concentrates; packaging materials such as polyethylene terephthalate ("PET"), bio-based PET and recycled PET for bottles; and aluminum cans, glass bottles and other containers.
Water is a main ingredient in substantially all of our products. KO.quantitative_report:
**Quantitative Report: Coca-Cola (KO) as of 2023-10-01**

**1. Revenue, Earnings, Margin, and Balance-Sheet Trajectory**
*   **Revenue:** TTM revenue is 44.14B. The quarterly path shows volatility: Q2 2023 (11.97B) grew +9.0% QoQ and +5.7% YoY, but Q4 2022 (10.12B) and Q1 2023 (10.98B) were sequentially lower.
*   **Earnings:** TTM net income is 10.51B. Q2 2023 EPS was 0.59, which is +34.1% YoY but -18.1% QoQ from Q1's 0.72. The YoY growth is strong; the QoQ decline indicates quarterly volatility.
*   **Margins:** Gross margin in Q2 2023 was 59.0% (7.06B gross profit / 11.97B revenue). Operating margin compressed to 20.1% (2.40B / 11.97B) from Q1's 30.7% (3.37B / 10.98B). TTM operating margin was 24.8% (10.93B / 44.14B).
*   **Balance Sheet & Leverage:** As of Q2 2023, total debt is 36.80B against total equity of 27.55B, resulting in a debt-to-equity of 1.336. Cash stands at 14.43B. Net debt (debt - cash) is 22.37B.

**2. Earnings Quality and Balance-Sheet Resilience**
*   **Earnings Quality:** The high TTM net margin of 23.8% and ROE of 38.2% indicate strong profitability. However, quarterly EPS volatility (0.46 to 0.72 over last 4 quarters) suggests variability in earnings power. Cash flow data is unavailable, preventing a direct assessment of cash conversion quality.
*   **Balance-Sheet Resilience:** The leverage is significant. A debt-to-equity ratio of 1.336 and net debt of 22.37B are key quantitative risks. Cash of 14.43B provides a buffer but is well below total debt levels.

**3. Valuation vs. Own History**
*   The latest P/E (diluted) is 23.1, which is at the low end of its observed one-year range of 23.0 to 29.2. Similarly, the EV/EBIT of 24.3 is at its one-year low (range: 24.2 to 29.0). Price/Book of 8.82 is also near the low of its range (8.8 to 11.4).
*   Valuation multiples have contracted from their 2023 highs (e.g., P/E peaked around 29.1 in April 2023) to current levels near their one-year lows.

**4. Price Level and Return Context vs. Fundamentals**
*   The share price on 2023-09-29 was 55.98, within a 52-week range of 54.02 to 64.99.
*   **Returns:** The stock returned -7.0% over the last quarter (~63 trading days from 2023-06-30) and -1.8% over the last year.
*   **Context vs. Fundamentals:** The quarterly price decline of -7.0% occurred alongside quarterly revenue growth of +9.0% but a QoQ decline in EPS of -18.1%. The annual price return of -1.8% contrasts with YoY revenue growth of +5.7% and strong YoY EPS growth of +34.1%.

**5. Strongest Quantitative Upside and Downside**
*   **Upside:** Valuation multiple re-expansion. The P/E and EV/EBIT are at their one-year lows. A reversion toward the mid-range (e.g., P/E 26-27) could provide upside, supported by a TTM net margin of 23.8% and ROE of 38.2%.
*   **Downside:** Leverage and margin compression risk. The debt-to-equity of 1.336 is high. Further operating margin compression (from 30.7% in Q1 2023 to 20.1% in Q2 2023) if sustained would pressure earnings and valuation. The stock is trading near its 52-week low (54.02).

**6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion**
Based on point-in-time data, the quantitative profile presents a mixed picture. The stock is trading at historically low valuation multiples (P/E 23.1, EV/EBIT 24.3) relative to its own one-year history, which constitutes a potential upside catalyst. However, this occurs alongside a high leverage ratio (debt-to-equity 1.336) and evidence of volatile margins (operating margin from 30.7% to 20.1%). The recent price underperformance (-7.0% quarterly, -1.8% annual) contrasts with generally positive growth metrics (YoY revenue +5.7%, YoY EPS +34.1%). The risk is that low multiples reflect justified concern over balance-sheet resilience and margin durability. The reward hinges on valuation mean-reversion and earnings stability. A definitive conclusion is limited by the absence of cash flow data.
KO.qualitative_report:
### Qualitative Assessment: The Coca-Cola Company (KO)
**Decision Date: 2023-10-01**

**1. Business/Franchise Read (from Supplied Narrative)**
Coca-Cola is a global total beverage company with a portfolio of over 200 countries and territories. Its core franchise is built on iconic brands (e.g., Coca-Cola, Sprite, Fanta), a vast distribution system powered by independent bottling partners, and a product mix spanning sparkling soft drinks, water, sports drinks, coffee, tea, and juices. The business model includes high-margin concentrate operations and lower-margin finished product operations. Its competitive strengths include leading brands, a sophisticated global marketing capability, and a large, stable bottling network. The company faces intense competition from players like PepsiCo, Nestlé, and Keurig Dr Pepper, as well as from private-label brands and e-commerce microbrands.

**2. Recent Developments (Dated, Supplied Evidence)**
*   **Guidance & Performance:** On 2023-08-30, a report indicated the company raised its full-year 2023 guidance, expecting organic revenue growth of 8% to 9% (up from 7% to 8%) and a higher earnings per share outlook, citing strong results and confidence for the rest of the year.
*   **Pricing Power & Demand:** On 2023-09-02, analysis highlighted Coca-Cola's demonstrated pricing power, noting it raised prices by 10% without sacrificing much demand. Core and non-traditional brands were reported to be performing well.
*   **Capital Allocation & Investor Narrative:** Multiple articles in September 2023 (e.g., 2023-09-28, 2023-09-29, 2023-09-30) consistently positioned Coca-Cola as a key holding for its dividend and as a core component of Warren Buffett's portfolio. It was cited as a "safe" stock amid low consumer confidence (2023-09-28).
*   **ESG & Sustainability Focus:** A 2023-09-30 article discussed the company's robust ESG strategy as an appeal for ethical investors.
*   **Competition:** A direct comparison on 2023-09-28 analyzed Coca-Cola versus PepsiCo, concluding investors "can't go wrong with either."
*   **Corporate Actions & Expansion:** On 2023-01-23 (referenced in 10-K), bottler Swire Coca-Cola acquired the company's bottling operations in Vietnam, expanding its system in Asia.

**3. Material Qualitative Risks and Offsets**
*   **Risks (Confirmed Evidence):**
    *   **Litigation & Regulatory Scrutiny:** The company faces ongoing lawsuits alleging false advertising and misleading labeling. This includes a suit over "natural" claims on Simply orange juice (2023-08-19), a suit over "100% recyclable" bottle claims (date not provided, but referenced 2023-09-28), and prior FDA correspondence regarding labeling (2023-08-28). A 2023-08-28 article noted the potential for more lawsuits as testing for contaminants like PFAS improves.
    *   **Competition & Market Dynamics:** The 10-K details a highly competitive landscape from global giants, regional players, private labels, and direct-to-consumer brands. A shifting digital landscape and powerful retail buyers are noted as challenges.
    *   **Health & Consumer Trends:** A strategic analysis (2023-08-12) identified a gap in providing enough healthy drink options to meet market trends, a potential long-term headwind.
*   **Offsets (Confirmed Evidence):**
    *   **Strong Brand & Pricing Power:** Evidence from 2023-09-02 directly confirms significant pricing power with limited demand destruction, a key offset to cost inflation.
    *   **Robust Global System & Scale:** The 10-K emphasizes the extensive bottler network and distribution capabilities as core strengths, enabling market reach and efficiency.
    *   **Strategic Agility & Portfolio Management:** The company is described as continually optimizing its portfolio (10-K) and has a strategic structure to quickly scale innovations across markets.
    *   **Shareholder Return Focus:** Consistent narrative positioning as a reliable dividend stock (multiple September 2023 articles) supports a defensive investment thesis.

**4. Concise Qualitative Assessment**
**Positive.** The supplied evidence points to a strengthening business narrative. The company has demonstrated pricing power, raised its forward guidance, and maintains a dominant franchise with strong brand equity. Its strategic focus on sustainability and innovation, coupled with its role as a core defensive holding, provides qualitative support. While litigation risks are confirmed and ongoing, they do not appear to have altered the fundamental business trajectory within the evidence window. The primary risks relate to long-term consumer shifts and fierce competition, which are inherent to the sector and partially offset by the company's scale and marketing prowess.

- [C3] The qual report states 'On 2023-08-30, a report indicated the company raised its full-year 2023 guidance, expecting organic revenue growth of 8% to 9% (up from 7% to 8%) and a higher earnings per share outlook.' The raw qualitative evidence confirms this via the 2023-08-30 article summary. However, the quant report does not incorporate this forward guidance upgrade into its analysis, which could support the case for revenue growth sustainability and valuation re-expansion. → The PM should note that the raised guidance (organic revenue growth 8-9%, up from 7-8%) provides forward-looking support for the quant report's upside thesis on valuation mean-reversion, but this was not cross-referenced between the two reports.

**Unsupported claims:**
- >The quant report states 'Cash flow data is unavailable' — this is accurate for the quant pack (all cash flow entries show 'no data'), but the qual pack contains a partial cash flow figure ($4.6B operating cash flow YTD) that was not shared across reports. This is not an unsupported claim per se but an omission of available evidence.
- The qual report's claim that 'the company raised its full-year 2023 guidance' is supported by the 2023-08-30 article in the raw evidence. No unsupported claims detected in the qualitative report.

pm_decision_obj.executive_summary: Coca-Cola's valuation has compressed to near one-year lows (P/E 23.1, EV/EBIT 24.3), and the company raised its 2023 guidance with demonstrated pricing power, but high leverage (D/E 1.34), volatile operating margins (20.1%–30.7%), and ongoing litigation risks limit conviction in a near-term re-rating. The risk/reward is roughly balanced with a mild positive tilt from valuation mean-reversion potential.
pm_decision_obj.investment_thesis: Coca-Cola's valuation has compressed to near one-year lows (P/E 23.1, EV/EBIT 24.3), and the company raised its 2023 guidance with demonstrated pricing power, but high leverage (D/E 1.34), volatile operating margins (20.1%–30.7%), and ongoing litigation risks limit conviction in a near-term re-rating. The risk/reward is roughly balanced with a mild positive tilt from valuation mean-reversion potential.
key_factors: Valuation at one-year lows: P/E of 23.1 and EV/EBIT of 24.3 sit at the bottom of their 1-year ranges (P/E 23.0–29.2, EV/EBIT 24.2–29.0), offering potential upside from mean-reversion toward mid-range multiples of 26–27.

Raised guidance and pricing power: On 2023-08-30, KO raised full-year 2023 organic revenue growth guidance to 8–9% from 7–8%, and on 2023-09-02 analysis confirmed 10% price increases with limited demand destruction, supporting earnings durability.

High leverage and margin volatility: Debt-to-equity of 1.336 with net debt of $22.4B is elevated, and operating margin compressed sharply from 30.7% in Q1 2023 to 20.1% in Q2 2023, creating downside risk if sustained.

Partial cash flow support: Qualitative evidence indicates $4.6B operating cash flow YTD as of mid-2023, partially offsetting the quant report's cash flow caveat and supporting earnings quality, though full cash conversion data remains incomplete.

Item 1. Business
The Boeing Company, together with its subsidiaries (herein referred to as "Boeing," the "Company," "we," "us," "our"), is one of the world's major aerospace firms.
We are organized based on the products and services we offer. We operate in four reportable segments:
- 
Commercial Airplanes (BCA);
- 
Defense, Space & Security (BDS);
- 
Global Services (BGS);
- 
Boeing Capital (BCC).
Commercial Airplanes Segment
This segment develops, produces and markets commercial jet aircraft principally to the commercial airline industry worldwide. We are a leading producer of commercial aircraft and offer a family of commercial jetliners designed to meet a broad spectrum of global passenger and cargo requirements of airlines. This family of commercial jet aircraft in production includes the 737 narrow-body model and the 767, 777 and 787 wide-body models. We ended production of the 747 wide-body model in 2022. Development continues on the 777X program and the 737-7 and 737-10 derivatives.
Defense, Space & Security Segment
This segment engages in the research, development, production and modification of manned and unmanned military aircraft and weapons systems for strike, surveillance and mobility, including fighter and trainer aircraft; vertical lift, including rotorcraft and tilt-rotor aircraft; and commercial derivative aircraft, including anti-submarine and tanker aircraft. In addition, this segment engages in the research, development, production and modification of the following products and related services: strategic defense and intelligence systems, including strategic missile and defense systems, command, control, communications, computers, intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance (C4ISR), cyber and information solutions, and intelligence systems, satellite systems, including government and commercial satellites and space exploration.
Global Services Segment
This segment provides services to our commercial and defense customers worldwide. BGS sustains aerospace platforms and systems with a full spectrum of products and services, including supply chain and logistics management, engineering, maintenance and modifications, upgrades and conversions, spare parts, pilot and maintenance training systems and services, technical and maintenance documents, and data analytics and digital services.
Boeing Capital Segment
BCC seeks to ensure that Boeing customers have the financing they need to buy and take delivery of their Boeing product, while managing overall financing exposure. BCC's portfolio consists of equipment under operating leases, sales-type/finance leases, notes and other receivables, assets held for sale or re-lease and investments.

Intellectual Property
We own numerous patents and

ion (FAA) and the Department of Homeland Security. Similar government authorities exist in our non-U.S. markets.
Government Contracts.
The U.S. government, and other governments, may terminate any of our government contracts at their convenience, as well as for default based on our failure to meet specified performance requirements. If any of our U.S. government contracts were to be terminated for convenience, we generally would be entitled to receive payment for work completed and allowable termination or cancellation costs. If any of our government contracts were to be terminated for default, generally the U.S. government would pay only for the work that has been accepted and could require us to pay the difference between the original contract price and the cost to re-procure the contract items, net of the work accepted from the original contract. The U.S. government can also hold us liable for damages resulting from the default.
Commercial Aircraft.
In the U.S., our commercial aircraft products are required to comply with FAA regulations governing production and quality systems, airworthiness and installation approvals, repair procedures and continuing operational safety. New aircraft models and new derivative aircraft are required to obtain FAA certification prior to entry into service. Outside the U.S., similar requirements exist for airworthiness, installation and operational approvals. These requirements are generally administered by the national aviation authorities of each country and, in the case of Europe, coordinated by the European Union Aviation Safety Agency.
Environmental.
We are subject to various federal, state, local and non-U.S. laws and regulations relating to environmental protection, including the discharge, treatment, storage, disposal and remediation of hazardous substances and wastes. We could also be affected by laws and regulations relating to climate change, including laws limiting or otherwise related to greenhouse gas emissions. These laws and regulations could lead to increased environmental compliance expenditures, increased energy and raw materials costs and new and/or additional investment in designs and technologies. We continually assess our compliance status and management of environmental matters to ensure our operations are in compliance with all applicable environmental laws and regulations. Investigation, remediation and operation and maintenance costs associated with environmental compliance and management of sites are a normal, recurring part of our operations. These costs often are allowable costs under our contracts with the U.S. government. It is reasonably possible that costs incurred to ensure continued environmental compliance could have a material impact on our results of operations, financial condition or cash flows if additional work requirements or more stringent clean-up standards are imposed by regulators, new areas of soil, air and groundwater contamination are discovered and/or expansions of work scope are prompted by the results of investigations.
For additional information relating to environmental contingencies, see Note 13 to our Consolidated Financial Statements.
Non-U.S. Sales.
Our non-U.S. sales are subject to both U.S. and non-U.S. governmental regulations and procurement policies and practices, including regulations relating to import-export control, tariffs, investment, exchange controls, anti-corruption and repatriation of earnings. Non-U.S. sales are also subject to varying currency, political and economic risks.
Raw Materials, Parts and Subassemblies
We are highly dependent on the availability of essential materials, parts and subassemblies from our suppliers and subcontractors. The most important raw materials required for our aerospace products
4
Table of Contents
are aluminum (sheet, plate, forgings and extrusions), titanium (sheet, plate, forgings and extrusions) and composites (including carbon and boron). Although alternative sources generally exist for these raw materials, qualification of the sources could take a year or more. During 2022, as a result of the Russia Ukraine war, we suspended purchasing titanium from Russia. This has not disrupted our operations as we have been able to use inventory on hand and identify alternative sources. Many major components and product equipment items are procured or subcontracted on a sole-source basis. We continue to work with a small number of sole-source suppliers to ensure continuity of supply for certain items.
Suppliers
We are dependent upon the ability of a large number of U.S. and non-U.S. suppliers and subcontractors to meet performance specifications, quality standards and delivery schedules at our anticipated costs. While we maintain an extensive qualification and performance surveillance system to control risk associated with such reliance on third parties, failure of suppliers or subcontractors to meet commitments could adversely affect production schedules and program/contract profitability, thereby jeopardizing our ability to fulfill commitments to our customers. We are also dependent on the availability of energy sources, such as electricity, at affordable prices.
Seasonality
No material portion of our business is considered to be seasonal.
Executive Officers of the Registrant
See "Item 10. Directors, Executive Officers and Corporate Governance" in Part III.
Other Information
Boeing was originally incorporated in the State of Washington in 1916 and reincorporated in Delaware in 1934. Our principal executive offices are located at 929 Long Bridge Drive, Arlington, Virginia 22202, and our telephone number is (703) 465-3500.
General information about us can be found at www.boeing.com. The information contained on or connected to our website is not incorporated by reference into this Annual Report on Form 10-K and should not be considered part of this or any other report filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC). Our Annual Report on Form 10-K, Quarterly Reports on Form 10-Q and Current Reports on Form 8-K, as well as any amendments to those reports, are available free of charge through our website as soon as reasonably practicable after we file them with, or furnish them to, the SEC. The SEC maintains a website at www.sec.gov that contains reports, proxy statements and other information regarding SEC registrants, including Boeing.
Forward-Looking Statements
This report, as well as our annual report to shareholders, quarterly reports and other filings we make with the SEC, press and earnings releases and other written and oral communications, contain "forward-looking statements" within the meaning of the Private Securities Litigation Reform Act of 1995. Words such as "may," "should," "expects," "intends," "projects," "plans," "believes," "estimates," "targets," "anticipates" and similar expressions generally identify these forward-looking statements. Examples of forward-looking statements include statements relating to our future financial condition and operating results, as well as any other statement that does not directly relate to any historical or current fact.

Any forward-looking statement speaks only as of the date on which it is made, and we assume no obligation to update or revise any forward-looking statement whether as a result of new information, future events or otherwise, except as required by law.
BA.quantitative_report:
### Quantitative Report: BA (Decision Date 2023-10-01)

**1. Revenue, Earnings, Margin, Cash-Flow, and Balance-Sheet Trajectory**
- **Revenue**: Q2 2023 revenue was $19.75B, representing a QoQ increase of +10.2% from Q1 2023 ($17.92B) and a YoY increase of +18.4% from Q2 2022 ($16.68B). TTM revenue (last 4 quarters) totals $73.61B.
- **Earnings**: Q2 2023 net income was -$149M (EPS -$0.25), a deterioration from Q1 2023's -$425M (EPS -$0.69). TTM net income is -$4.54B. EPS has been negative in 6 of the last 8 quarters.
- **Margins**: Q2 2023 gross margin was 9.8% ($1.94B / $19.75B). Operating margin was -0.5% (-$99M / $19.75B). TTM net margin is -6.2% (-$4.54B / $73.61B).
- **Cash-Flow**: Data unavailable for the last 8 quarters.
- **Balance Sheet**: As of Q2 2023, total equity is negative at -$15.49B. Total debt is $52.04B. Debt has declined from $62.24B in Q3 2021. Cash balances are not reported for Q2 2023; the last available was $14.30B in Q3 2022.

**2. Earnings Quality and Balance-Sheet Resilience**
- **Earnings Quality**: Earnings are poor. TTM operating income is -$3.40B. The net margin has been negative for the entire 365-day valuation window (range: -10% to -13.9%). The QoQ revenue growth of +10.2% in Q2 2023 was accompanied by a -63.8% drop in EPS, indicating high operating leverage and cost pressures.
- **Balance-Sheet Resilience**: The balance sheet is strained. Total equity has been negative since at least Q3 2021 (-$14.27B), declining to -$15.49B by Q2 2023. The company is technically insolvent. Debt-to-asset ratio (total debt/total assets) is 0.39 ($52.04B / $134.77B).

**3. Valuation versus Own History**
- The only available valuation metric is Price-to-Sales (P/S). As of 2023-09-29, P/S is 1.576.
- **Comparison**: No historical P/S range is provided. However, net margin has improved from -13.9% (Nov 2022) to -6.2% (Sep 2023), suggesting some fundamental improvement, but P/E and EV/EBIT are unavailable due to negative earnings.

**4. Price Level and Quarterly/Annual Return Context**
- **Price**: Close on 2023-09-29 was $191.68. The 52-week range was $120.99 to $243.10.
- **Returns**: Quarterly (Q3 2023) return was -9.2%. Annual (since Sep 2022) return was +43.6%.
- **Context**: The +43.6% annual price gain occurred alongside a TTM net loss of -$4.54B and a persistently negative equity. The recent quarterly decline of -9.2% coincides with continued losses in Q2 2023.

**5. Strongest Quantitative Upside and Downside**
- **Upside**: Continued revenue growth (TTM $73.61B, YoY +18.4% in latest quarter) and debt reduction (from $62.24B to $52.04B) could improve financial flexibility. Gross margin stabilized near 10% in recent quarters.
- **Downside**: Persistent negative earnings (TTM net income -$4.54B) and negative equity (-$15.49B) pose solvency risk. Operating cash flow is undisclosed, and profitability remains elusive despite revenue growth.

**6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion**
- The quantitative picture is conflicted. Strong revenue growth and annual price appreciation are offset by deep, persistent losses, negative equity, and undisclosed cash flow. The stock has rallied 43.6% in a year despite a -4.54B TTM net loss, implying the market is pricing in a recovery not yet visible in the income statement. The negative equity and lack of earnings make valuation speculative. Risk is elevated due to the deteriorated balance sheet; reward depends entirely on a future return to profitability not evidenced in the supplied data.
BA.qualitative_report:
**Qualitative Report: Boeing (BA)**
**Decision Date:** 2023-10-01

**1. Business/Franchise Read from Supplied Narrative**
From the 10-K filing (2023-01-27), Boeing is a major global aerospace company with a diversified franchise across four segments:
*   **Commercial Airplanes (BCA):** A leading producer of jetliners (737, 767, 777, 787), competing intensely with Airbus and new entrants from China.
*   **Defense, Space & Security (BDS):** Produces military aircraft, weapons systems, and satellites, competing with major U.S. contractors like Lockheed Martin, Northrop Grumman, and Raytheon.
*   **Global Services (BGS):** Provides aftermarket parts, maintenance, and digital services for commercial and defense platforms.
*   **Boeing Capital (BCC):** Provides financing to customers.

The business is heavily regulated (FAA, U.S. government agencies) and has a significant unionized workforce. Boeing highlights its dependence on a complex global supply chain and sole-source suppliers for critical components.

**2. Recent Developments (Dated Evidence Only)**
*   **Commercial Order Momentum (Positive):**
    *   **2023-09-26:** Air Canada converts a previous freighter order to 787-10 Dreamliners, indicating continued demand for the widebody program.
    *   **2023-09-28:** Report notes Air Canada's order for 18 787-10 Dreamliners as part of fleet modernization.
*   **Long-Term Growth Outlook (Positive):**
    *   **2023-09-19, 2023-09-20:** Boeing forecasts China will need 8,560 new commercial airplanes through 2042 due to strong economic and air travel growth.
    *   **2023-09-26:** Boeing and Airbus both forecast Asian leadership in future air travel growth.
*   **Defense & Government Activity:**
    *   **2023-09-26:** Boeing secures a contract to supply support equipment and inspection kits for the U.S. Navy's F/A-18E/F and EA-18G aircraft.
    *   **2023-09-27:** A report links increased U.S. military aid to Ukraine with a boost to the U.S. industrial economy, a potential tailwind for the defense segment.
*   **Legal/Regulatory Settlement (Negative):**
    *   **2023-09-28:** Boeing agrees to pay an **$8.1 million** settlement to resolve allegations it violated the False Claims Act related to manufacturing specifications for V-22 Osprey military aircraft parts.

**3. Material Qualitative Risks and Offsets**
*   **Risk: Execution & Production Quality.** The 10-K emphasizes heavy regulation and quality requirements. The recent (2023-09-28) False Claims Act settlement underscores ongoing execution and compliance risks in its defense production.
*   **Risk: Intense Competition.** The 10-K explicitly states competition from Airbus in commercial and from multiple large contractors (Lockheed, Northrop, etc.) in defense is "extremely competitive" and expected to continue. Chinese entrants are also noted.
*   **Risk: Supply Chain & Regulatory Disruption.** The 10-K details reliance on sole-source suppliers and the need for FAA/CASA certification. Disruptions or failures in these areas can impact delivery schedules.
*   **Offset: Secular Demand & Backlog.** Recent news (Air Canada order, China forecast) reinforces the long-term, fundamental demand narrative for commercial air travel, particularly in Asia, which supports the BCA segment's backlog and future delivery outlook.
*   **Offset: Defense Diversification.** The consistent flow of smaller defense contracts (e.g., F/A-18 support) and the beneficial impact of geopolitical factors on U.S. industrial activity provide stability and diversification away from commercial cycles.

**4. Concise Qualitative Assessment**
**Cautiously Positive.** The recent narrative (August-September 2023) is skewed positive for the commercial franchise, with concrete order activity and a bullish multi-decade demand outlook from key growth markets like China. This suggests a durable recovery path for the core BCA segment. However, this is balanced against persistent, material risks: operational execution and quality (highlighted by the latest legal settlement), intense competition, and supply chain fragility. The stock's sentiment appears to be shifting toward optimism on growth catalysts, but the underlying business risks are severe and well-documented. The overall qualitative picture is one of a franchise showing tangible signs of commercial momentum amid a still-challenging operational environment.

The raw balance sheet confirms cash=14.30B only at 2022-09-30, with no cash data for subsequent quarters. This is correctly flagged as a gap. corrections.required_action: Consider noting the declining cash trend (from $20.0B in Q3 2021 to $14.3B in Q3 2022) as additional balance-sheet risk context.

unsupported_claims: : The quant report's statement 'The company is technically insolvent' is a characterization that goes beyond the raw data. The raw pack shows negative equity (-$15.49B), which is a necessary but not sufficient condition for technical insolvency (which typically requires inability to meet obligations as they come due). The report should state 'negative equity' rather than 'technically insolvent' without further evidence on liquidity or debt maturity schedules.

: The qual report's framing of the False Claims Act settlement as underscoring 'ongoing execution and compliance risks' is a reasonable inference but the raw evidence only states the settlement amount and the nature of the violation (failure to adhere to manufacturing specifications for V-22 Osprey parts). The characterization is defensible but slightly interpretive.

pm_decision_obj.executive_summary: Boeing's revenue recovery is genuine (TTM $73.6B, +18.4% YoY in Q2 2023) and long-term demand catalysts are real (China forecast of 8,560 new aircraft through 2042, Air Canada 787 order), but the stock has already rallied +43.6% over the past year, pricing in much of the turnaround. With persistent TTM losses of -$4.54B, negative equity of -$15.49B, and no cash-flow visibility, the risk/reward is roughly balanced at current levels.
pm_decision_obj.investment_thesis: Boeing's revenue recovery is genuine (TTM $73.6B, +18.4% YoY in Q2 2023) and long-term demand catalysts are real (China forecast of 8,560 new aircraft through 2042, Air Canada 787 order), but the stock has already rallied +43.6% over the past year, pricing in much of the turnaround. With persistent TTM losses of -$4.54B, negative equity of -$15.49B, and no cash-flow visibility, the risk/reward is roughly balanced at current levels.
key_factors: Revenue momentum is real but already priced in: TTM revenue of $73.61B with Q2 2023 YoY growth of +18.4%, yet the stock has already appreciated +43.6% over the past year, suggesting the market has priced in much of the recovery.

Balance sheet remains structurally impaired: negative equity of -$15.49B, total debt of $52.04B, TTM net losses of -$4.54B, and declining cash balances (from $20.0B in Q3 2021 to $14.3B in Q3 2022) with no Q2 2023 cash data available.

Long-term demand narrative provides a floor: Boeing forecasts China will need 8,560 new commercial airplanes through 2042, and recent orders like Air Canada's 18 787-10 Dreamliners (2023-09-28) confirm commercial momentum.
Execution and legal risks persist: the $8.1M False Claims Act settlement (2023-09-28) for V-22 Osprey parts underscores ongoing compliance and quality-control challenges in defense production.

Latest 10-K business section (newest first):
  filing_date=2023-02-10 section=business period_end=2022-12-31
ITEM 1.    BUSINESS
Overview
American Express is a globally integrated payments company, providing customers with access to products, insights and experiences that enrich lives and build business success. We are a leader in providing credit and charge cards to consumers, small businesses, mid-sized companies and large corporations around the world. American Express® cards issued by us, as well as by third-party banks and other institutions on the American Express network, can be used by Card Members to charge purchases at the millions of merchants around the world that accept cards bearing our logo.
Our various products and services are offered globally to diverse customer groups through various channels, including mobile and online applications, affiliate marketing, customer referral programs, third-party service providers and business partners, direct mail, telephone, in-house sales teams and direct response advertising.
We were founded in 1850 as a joint stock association and were incorporated in 1965 as a New York corporation. American Express Company and its principal operating subsidiary, American Express Travel Related Services Company, Inc. (TRS), are bank holding companies under the Bank Holding Company Act of 1956, as amended (the BHC Act), subject to supervision and examination by the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System (the Federal Reserve).
We principally engage in businesses comprising four reportable operating segments: U.S. Consumer Services (USCS), Commercial Services (CS), International Card Services (ICS) and Global Merchant and Network Services (GMNS). Corporate functions and certain other businesses are included in Corporate & Other. Our businesses function together to form our end-to-end integrated payments platform, which we believe is a differentiator that underpins our business model. For further information about our reportable operating segments, please see "Business Segment Results of Operations" under "MD&A."

Our Integrated Payments Platform
Through our general-purpose card-issuing, merchant-acquiring and card network businesses, we are able to connect participants and provide differentiated value across the commerce path. We maintain direct relationships with both our Card Members (as a card issuer) and merchants (as an acquirer), and we handle all key aspects of those relationships. These relationships create a "closed loop" in that we have direct access to information at both ends of the card transaction, which distinguishes our integrated payments platform from the bankcard networks.

COMPETITION
We compete in the global payments industry with card networks, issuers and acquirers, paper-based transactions (e.g., cash and checks), bank transfer models (e.g., wire transfers and Automated Clearing House, or ACH), as well as evolving and growing alternative mechanisms, systems and products that leverage new technologies, business models and customer relationships to create payment, financing or banking solutions. The payments industry continues to undergo dynamic changes in response to evolving technologies, consumer habits and merchant needs, some of which have accelerated as a result of the pandemic, such as an increased shift to digital payments.
As a card issuer, we compete with financial institutions that issue general-purpose credit and debit cards. We also encounter competition from businesses that issue private label cards, operate mobile wallets or extend credit. We face intense competition in the premium space and for cobrand relationships, as both card issuer and network competitors have targeted high-spending customers and key business partners with attractive value propositions. We also face competition for partners and other differentiated offerings, such as lounge space in U.S. and global hub airports.

AXP.quantitative_report:
**Quantitative Report: AXP as of 2023-10-01**

**1. Financial Trajectory**
*   **Revenue & Earnings Growth:** TTM revenue (Q2 2023) is $36.09B. The latest quarter (Q2 2023) shows revenue of $9.43B, with QoQ growth of +6.6% and YoY growth of +7.5%. Diluted EPS in Q2 2023 is $2.89, with QoQ growth of +20.4% and YoY growth of +12.5%. TTM net income is $7.44B.
*   **Margins:** The latest operating margin (Q2 2023) is approximately 28.9% (calculated: $2.73B / $9.43B). TTM operating income is $9.23B. The latest reported net margin is 0.206 (as of 2023-09-29), which has compressed from 0.24 in October 2022.
*   **Cash Flow:** Cash flow statements are unavailable for all periods provided.
*   **Balance Sheet:** Total assets have grown from $184.26B (2021-09-30) to $244.90B (2023-06-30). Total equity has grown modestly from $24.43B to $26.70B over the same period. Total debt has increased from $34.48B to $46.73B.

**2. Earnings Quality & Balance-Sheet Resilience**
*   **Earnings Quality:** Net margin has declined from 0.24 to 0.206 over the past year. ROE has declined from 0.327 (2022-10-03) to 0.279 (2023-09-29). The absence of cash flow data precludes analysis of cash conversion.
*   **Balance-Sheet Resilience:** The debt-to-equity ratio is 1.75 as of the latest valuation. Total equity ($26.70B) is substantially smaller than total assets ($244.90B), reflecting the company's leveraged financial model. The trend shows total debt increasing by $12.25B from Q3 2021 to Q2 2023, while total equity increased by only $2.27B.

**3. Valuation vs. Supplied History**
*   The latest P/E (diluted) is 14.857, which sits at the lower end of its 365-day observed range of 13.5 to 18.4. The latest P/B ratio is 4.14, down from a high of 5.6 in the past year. The earnings yield is 6.7%. Valuation metrics are unavailable for price-to-cash-flow.

**4. Price Level and Return Context**
*   **Price Level:** The latest closing price is $149.19 (2023-09-29). The 52-week high is $182.15 and the low is $130.65.
*   **Return Context:** The quarterly price delta (approx. 63 trading days to 2023-09-29) is -14.4%. The annual price delta (approx. 252 trading days) is +6.1%. This recent price decline has occurred during a period of reported QoQ and YoY growth in revenue and EPS.

**5. Strongest Quantitative Upside and Downside**
*   **Upside:** The stock is trading near the bottom of its one-year P/E valuation range (14.857 vs. 13.5 low). Recent quarterly growth in revenue (+6.6%) and EPS (+20.4%) is robust.
*   **Downside:** Net margin and ROE have deteriorated over the past year. Leverage, as measured by debt-to-equity (1.75), has increased. The absence of cash flow data represents a critical information gap for assessing fundamental health.

**6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion**
*   The numerical evidence presents a mixed picture. There is growth in revenue and earnings, and valuation multiples are at the lower end of recent ranges. However, this is offset by deteriorating margin and return metrics, a more leveraged balance sheet, and a complete lack of cash flow data to validate earnings quality. The recent sharp price decline (-14.4% quarterly) has not been preceded by a deterioration in the reported earnings trajectory, creating a potential quantitative disconnect, but the fundamental trends on margins and leverage are cautionary. A definitive risk/reward assessment is materially hampered by the unavailable cash flow evidence.
AXP.qualitative_report:
**Qualitative Assessment Report: American Express (AXP)**
**Decision Date: 2023-10-01**

### 1. Business/Franchise Read
American Express is a globally integrated payments company operating a "closed-loop" model, which directly connects card members and merchants. This model provides proprietary data on both ends of transactions, differentiating it from bankcard networks. The business is structured around four segments: U.S. Consumer Services, Commercial Services, International Card Services, and Global Merchant & Network Services. Its "spend-centric" model prioritizes generating revenue from high spending volumes rather than solely from finance charges, supported by a valuable brand, premium products, and a large, diverse customer base spanning consumers, SMEs, and large corporations globally. Key strategic partners, including Delta Air Lines, are vital to its value proposition.

### 2. Dated Recent Developments
*   **2023-09-30:** Industry analysis suggests multiple growth catalysts ahead for the fintech sector, in which AXP operates.
*   **2023-09-29:** An article listed recent analyst ratings for AXP, showing a distribution from "Bullish" to "Bearish" over the prior quarter, indicating a lack of consensus.
*   **2023-09-28:** Delta Air Lines' CEO stated the airline "probably went too far" with recent restrictions on its SkyMiles program, signaling a potential relaxation of rules. This is relevant as Delta is AXP's largest strategic cobrand partner.
*   **2023-09-26:** AXP announced it is set to add a mobile check deposit feature to a Business Blueprint product, part of ongoing technology investments to expand its card member base.
*   **2023-08-28:** A report noted that pandemic-driven behavioral changes, such as reduced business travel and event spending, caused corporate spending to remain down significantly from 2019 levels.

### 3. Material Qualitative Risks and Offsets
*   **Risk - Legal/Regulatory:** A class action lawsuit filed on **2023-09-28** alleges AXP submits hard credit inquiries. A Supreme Court decision noted on **2023-09-07** established legal reasoning for "two-sided platforms" that could be used by large tech companies, potentially shaping future regulatory or competitive landscapes.
*   **Risk - Partner Concentration:** The franchise is significantly linked to its Delta Air Lines partnership (approx. 10% of network volumes and 21% of loans as of **2022-12-31**). Changes to that partnership's strategy, such as the SkyMiles adjustments reported on **2023-09-28**, represent a key dependency.
*   **Risk - Economic Sensitivity:** The "spend-centric" model ties revenue closely to consumer and commercial spending volumes. Evidence from **2023-08-28** highlights that corporate spending has not fully recovered, exposing the business to macroeconomic shifts.
*   **Offset - Integrated Platform & Brand:** The closed-loop data advantage and global brand provide a durable competitive moat, enabling targeted marketing, risk management, and premium pricing. This is a core strength cited in the **2023-02-10** 10-K.
*   **Offset - Strategic Execution:** Recent actions like technology investments (**2023-09-26**) and a stated focus on premium consumers and commercial payments demonstrate ongoing efforts to execute on growth strategies outlined in the **2023-02-10** business description.

### 4. Concise Qualitative Assessment
**Neutral-to-Slightly Positive.** American Express possesses a strong, differentiated franchise with a powerful brand and integrated business model that serves as a durable competitive advantage. Recent corporate actions indicate ongoing investment in products and technology. However, the near-term narrative is tempered by persistent risks, including legal scrutiny, high dependence on a key partner, and sensitivity to a spending environment that has not fully normalized, particularly in commercial travel. Analyst sentiment shown in the supplied data is mixed. The core business appears resilient, but the path forward involves navigating these specific, evidenced risks.

corrections.issue: Quant report states 'The latest reported net margin is 0.206 (as of 2023-09-29), which has compressed from 0.24 in October 2022.' The raw valuation data shows net_margin=0.24 on 2022-10-03 and net_margin=0.206 on 2023-09-29, which is consistent. However, the quant report also states 'The latest operating margin (Q2 2023) is approximately 28.9% (calculated: $2.73B / $9.43B).' The raw valuation ratios show operating_margin=0.256 as of 2023-09-29. The 28.9% figure is a single-quarter calculation while 0.256 appears to be a trailing measure; these are not directly comparable and the report does not clarify the distinction, which could mislead on margin level.
corrections.required_action: Clarify that the 28.9% is a single-quarter operating margin while the valuation panel's 0.256 (25.6%) is likely a trailing measure; note the difference explicitly to avoid confusion.

corrections.issue: Quant report states 'Total equity has grown modestly from $24.43B to $26.70B' comparing 2021-09-30 to 2023-06-30. The raw balance sheet shows total_equity=24.43B on 2021-09-30 and total_equity=26.70B on 2023-06-30, which is correct. However, the intermediate trajectory shows equity dipped to 22.18B in 2021-12-31 before recovering, so the 'modest growth' framing obscures a period of equity decline. This is a minor omission but worth noting for completeness.
corrections.required_action: No action required; the claim is numerically accurate. The intermediate dip is available in the balance sheet data for the PM to review.

corrections.issue: Qual report states 'corporate spending has not fully recovered' citing 2023-08-28 article. The raw evidence states 'corporate spending to remain down two-thirds from where it was in 2019.' The qual report's characterization is directionally consistent but understates the severity implied by 'down two-thirds.' This is a minor softening of the evidence.
corrections.required_action: No action required; the qualitative characterization is directionally accurate though slightly softer than the raw source language.

unsupported_claims: Quant report states 'The latest reported net margin is 0.206 (as of 2023-09-29), which has compressed from 0.24 in October 2022.' This is supported by the raw valuation data (net_margin=0.24 on 2022-10-03, net_margin=0.206 on 2023-09-29). No issue.
Quant report states 'ROE has declined from 0.327 (2022-10-03) to 0.279 (2023-09-29).' This is supported by the raw valuation data (roe=0.327 on 2022-10-03, roe=0.279 on 2023-09-29). No issue.

Qual report references 'a class action lawsuit filed on 2023-09-28 alleges AXP submits hard credit inquiries.' The raw risk evidence confirms this: 'American Express class action alleges company submits hard credit...' on 2023-09-28. Supported.

Qual report states Delta cobrand portfolio 'approx. 10% of network volumes and 21% of loans as of 2022-12-31.' The 10-K business section confirms: 'the Delta cobrand portfolio represented approximately 10 percent of worldwide network volumes and approximately 21 percent of worldwide Card Member loans as of December 31, 2022.' Supported.

ITEM 1.    Business
OVERVIEW
Visa is one of the world's leaders in digital payments. Our purpose is to uplift everyone, everywhere by being the best way to pay and be paid. We facilitate global commerce and money movement across more than 200 countries and territories among a global set of consumers, merchants, financial institutions and government entities through innovative technologies.
Since Visa's early days in 1958, we have been in the business of facilitating payments between consumers and businesses. As a trusted engine of commerce and with new ways to pay, we are working to provide payment solutions for everyone, everywhere. We are focused on extending, enhancing and investing in our proprietary network, VisaNet, to offer a single connection point for facilitating payment transactions to multiple endpoints through various form factors. Through our network, we offer products, solutions and services that facilitate secure, reliable and efficient money movement for participants in the ecosystem.
- We facilitate secure, reliable and efficient money movement among consumers, issuing and acquiring financial institutions, and merchants. We have traditionally referred to this as the "four-party" model. Please see Our Core Business discussion below. As the payments ecosystem continues to evolve, we have broadened this model to include digital banks, digital wallets and a range of financial technology companies (fintechs), governments and non-governmental organizations (NGOs). We provide transaction processing services (primarily authorization, clearing and settlement) to our financial institution and merchant clients through VisaNet, our advanced transaction processing network. During fiscal year 2022, we saw 258 billion payments and cash transactions with Visa's brand, equating to an average of 707 million transactions per day. Of the 258 billion total transactions, 193 billion were processed by Visa.
- We offer a wide range of Visa-branded payment products that our 
…[truncated 20015 chars]…
 added value to our clients and their customers, financial institutions, merchants and partners through compelling brand expressions, a wide range of products and services as well as innovative brand and marketing efforts. In line with our commitment to an expansive and diverse range of partnerships for the benefit of our stakeholders, Visa is the only brand in the world that is a top sponsor of FIFA, the
11
Table of Contents
Olympic Games, and the NFL and is one of the most active sponsors of women's football around the world.
Talent
Attracting, developing and advancing the best talent globally is critical to our continued success. This year we grew our total workforce from approximately 21,500 in fiscal year 2021 to approximately 26,500 employees in fiscal year 2022, an increase of 23 percent year over year. Voluntary workforce turnover (rolling 12-month attrition) was 12.1 percent as of September 30, 2022. Visa employees are located in more than 80 countries and territories, with 54 percent located outside the U.S. At the end of fiscal year 2022, Visa's global workforce was 58 percent men and 42 percent women, and women represented 36 percent of Visa's leadership (defined as vice president level and above). In the U.S., ethnicity of our workforce was 41 percent Asian, 8 percent Black, 12 percent Hispanic, 3 percent Other and 36 percent White. For our U.S. leadership, the breakdown was 18 percent Asian, 7 percent Black, 13 percent Hispanic, 2 percent Other and 60 percent White.
Given Visa's ambitious growth agenda, it is important to enable our employees to achieve their individual performance goals while also supporting personal career interests. This year we introduced several changes to career growth and planning at Visa, including new growth paths and tools that take into consideration the unique professional backgrounds, skills, accomplishments, and future performance goals of our employees. These tools support meaningful dialogue about performance and help drive development, retention and growth of top talent in a highly competitive talent market.
We have an unwavering commitment to valuing the unique identities of our employees and their contributions to Visa. In 2020, we established the Stand Together initiative in support of social justice and racial equality in the U.S. focused on our people, our community and our company. We are proud of the progress we have made. Our partnership with the Thurgood Marshall College Fund for the Visa Black Scholars and Jobs Program resulted in Visa's inaugural class of 51 scholars participating in year-round programs and training aimed at developing their professional and technical skills this past year. We also welcomed the second cohort of 75 scholars this fall. Upon graduation, all scholars who have met their commitments will be offered a full-time job with Visa.
We continue to increase the number of underrepresented employees in the U.S. We are committed to recruiting and retaining diverse talent through employee development programs aimed at advancing their careers at Visa. As a company, we continue to partner with historically Black colleges and a generally more diverse set of universities to further develop our talent pipeline. Visa is committed to pay equity, regardless of gender or race/ethnicity, and conducts pay equity analyses on an annual basis. More details regarding our human capital management, as well as enhanced workforce disclosures that include our 2021 Consolidated EEO-1 Report and our 2021 Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG) Report, can be found on our website at visa.com/esg. See Available Information below.
For additional information, please see the section titled "Talent and Human Capital Management" in Visa's 2022 Proxy Statement.
FINTECH AND DIGITAL PARTNERSHIPS
Fintechs are key enablers of new payment experiences and new flows. Our work with fintechs has opened new points of acceptance, extended credit at the point of sale, made cross-border money flows more efficient, moved B2B spend onto Visa's network, expedited payroll and provided digital wallet customers access to our services.
To better serve fintechs, Visa has a suite of streamlined commercial programs and digital onboarding tools. Our Fintech Fast Track program enables qualifying fintechs to quickly launch and scale their programs. The program has welcomed hundreds of fintechs who are actively engaged in the program.
With our startup engagement programs, the Visa Everywhere Initiative and the Inclusive Fintech 50, early-stage companies can build payment solutions based on our capabilities. With the global Visa Fintech Partner Connect program, we are helping our clients tap into innovations emerging from the fintech community. The program is designed to help financial institutions quickly connect with a curated set of technology providers, helping Visa's issuing partners create digital-first experiences.
MERGERS AND ACQUISITIONS, JOINT VENTURES AND STRATEGIC INVESTMENTS
Visa continually explores opportunities to augment our capabilities and provide meaningful value to our clients. Mergers and acquisitions, joint ventures and strategic investments complement our internal development and
12
Table of Contents
enhance our partnerships to align with Visa's priorities. Visa applies a rigorous business analysis to our acquisitions, joint ventures and investments to ensure they will differentiate our network, provide value added services and accelerate growth.
In fiscal year 2022, we acquired The Currency Cloud Group Limited (Currencycloud), a global platform that enables financial institutions and fintechs to provide innovative, cross-border foreign exchange solutions. We also acquired Tink, an open banking platform that enables financial institutions, fintechs and merchants to build financial products and services and move money.
ENVIRONMENTAL, SOCIAL AND GOVERNANCE
As a trusted brand, Visa has an opportunity and responsibility to contribute to a more inclusive, equitable and sustainable world. We believe that economies that include everyone everywhere, uplift everyone everywhere. As we build business resilience and long-term value, we are committed to managing the risks and opportunities that arise from ESG issues. We are focused on empowering people and economies; securing commerce and protecting customers; investing in our workforce; protecting the planet; and operating responsibly. Our 2021 ESG report, as well as other ESG-related resources are available on our website at visa.com/esg. See Available Information below.
INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY
We own and manage the Visa brand, which stands for acceptance, security, convenience, speed and reliability. Our portfolio of Visa-owned trademarks is important to our business. Generally, trademark registrations are valid indefinitely as long as they are in use and/or maintained. We give our clients access to these assets through agreements with our issuers and acquirers, which authorize the use of our trademarks in connection with their participation in our payments network. Additionally, we own a number of patents and patent applications related to our business and continue to pursue patents in emerging technologies that may have applications in our business. We rely on a combination of patent, trademark, copyright and trade secret laws in the U.S. and other jurisdictions, as well as confidentiality procedures and contractual provisions, to protect our proprietary technology.
COMPETITION
The global payments industry continues to undergo dynamic change. Existing and emerging competitors compete with Visa's network and payment solutions for consumers and for participation by financial institutions and merchants. Technology and innovation are shifting consumer habits and driving growth opportunities in ecommerce, mobile payments, blockchain technology and digital currencies. These advances are enabling new entrants, many of which depa

V.quantitative_report:
**Quantitative Report: V (Visa)**
**Decision Date: 2023-10-01**

**1. Trajectory**
*   **Revenue:** Consistent growth. Quarterly revenue increased from $6.56B (Q4 2021) to $8.12B (Q3 2023). Latest YoY growth was +11.7%, QoQ growth was +1.7%. TTM revenue is $31.83B.
*   **Earnings:** Net income grew YoY by +21.8% to $4.16B in Q3 2023, but declined -2.4% QoQ. TTM net income is $16.53B.
*   **Margins:** High and stable. Net margin ranged from 0.503 to 0.519 over the trailing period. Latest operating margin is 0.645.
*   **Cash Flow:** Data unavailable for all reported periods.
*   **Balance Sheet:** Total assets grew from $82.90B (Q3 2021) to $89.23B (Q3 2023). Total equity increased from $37.59B to $38.98B. Total liabilities also grew, from $45.31B to $50.25B.

**2. Earnings Quality & Balance-Sheet Resilience**
*   **Earnings Quality:** High profitability margins (net margin ~0.52, ROE ~0.424) indicate strong earning power. However, cash flow generation is not quantifiable from the supplied data, precluding an assessment of earnings-to-cash conversion.
*   **Balance-Sheet Resilience:** Equity base is substantial at $38.98B. Liability growth ($45.31B to $50.25B) has outpaced asset growth in recent quarters, increasing financial leverage. The latest debt figure available is from Q4 2021 ($20.92B), which represented a debt-to-equity ratio of ~0.54.

**3. Valuation vs. History**
*   Supplied valuation ratios are limited to profitability metrics. Net margin and ROE have been stable within a narrow band (net_margin: 0.503-0.519; roe: 0.409-0.424) over the trailing year.
*   **Critical Gap:** Price-to-earnings (P/E), EV/EBIT, price-to-book (P/B), and free cash flow yield data are explicitly marked as **n/a**. Therefore, a direct valuation comparison to the company's own earnings or book value history is not possible with the supplied evidence.

**4. Price Level & Returns vs. Fundamentals**
*   **Price Level:** Closed at $230.01 on 2023-09-29, within a 52-week range of $174.60 to $250.58.
*   **Returns:** The stock generated a **+28.4% annual return** (vs. 2022-09-28) and a **-3.1% quarterly return** (vs. 2023-06-30). The strong annual return correlates with the period of reported revenue and net income growth.

**5. Strongest Quantitative Upside & Downside**
*   **Upside:** Demonstrated and consistent revenue growth (+11.7% YoY) coupled with industry-leading operating margins (0.645) and returns on equity (0.424). Strong annual price momentum (+28.4%).
*   **Downside:** Lack of cash flow data is a material evidence gap. Rising liabilities ($50.25B) against static debt figures creates an incomplete picture of financial risk. Recent quarterly price return is negative (-3.1%), and valuation cannot be assessed due to missing multiples.

**6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion**
The fundamental trajectory shows strong revenue growth and elite, stable profitability margins. However, the supplied evidence contains a critical gap: **cash flow data is entirely missing**, and key valuation multiples (P/E, P/B) are unavailable. Balance sheet analysis is incomplete without current debt figures. The strong annual price return aligns with fundamental growth, but the recent quarterly drawdown (-3.1%) introduces caution. A definitive risk/reward assessment cannot be made without cash flow data and valuation metrics. The quantitative profile is one of high profitability with incomplete evidence on cash generation and valuation.
V.qualitative_report:
### Qualitative Assessment: Visa Inc. (V)

**1. Business/Franchise Read (from 10-K Filing Date 2022-11-16)**
Visa is a global leader in digital payments, positioned as a critical four-party payment network (VisaNet) facilitating transactions among consumers, merchants, financial institutions, and government entities. The company does not issue cards or extend credit but earns revenues by processing transactions. Its strategy focuses on three growth pillars: **1) Consumer Payments** (credit, debit, prepaid), **2) New Flows** (e.g., Visa Direct for real-time P2P, B2C, and B2B payments), and **3) Value-Added Services** (issuing, acceptance, risk, and identity solutions). The business is fortified by its scale (4.1 billion credentials, $14 trillion in payments volume in FQ2022), powerful brand (top sponsor of FIFA, Olympics, NFL), and an evolving strategy to become a "network of networks" that enables money movement across diverse networks and form factors.

**2. Recent Developments (Dated from Supplied Text)**
*   **Positive Sentiment & Growth Narrative:** Articles dated **2023-09-30** highlight Visa as a "perfect growth stock" and a "money-making machine" primed for sustained success. Web context from **2023-09-10** notes Visa has exceeded recent market EPS projections.
*   **Financial Performance & Outlook:** Web context from **2023-08-24** reports that in a previous quarter, Visa beat the Zacks Consensus Estimate for revenue ($8.1 billion, +12% YoY) and earnings per share (+9% YoY). A news summary from **2023-08-28** cites CEO Al Kelly stating confidence in Visa's "long-term growth trajectory across consumer payments, new flows and value added services" despite short-term uncertainty.
*   **Capital Allocation:** A news summary from **2023-08-28** references Visa increasing its dividend.
*   **Competitive & Industry Context:** Items dated **2023-09-29** and **2023-09-30** frame fintech as an industry with many catalysts, with Visa positioned within this broader trend. An analyst note for a peer (Mastercard) dated **2023-09-25** highlights structural e-payment growth potential.
*   **Legal & Regulatory Activity:** A **2023-09-07** article reports that a court invalidated certain patents asserted against Visa in a secure payment case. However, a key risk materialized on **2023-09-03**, when a U.S. appeal court reopened a lawsuit by payment processor Pulse alleging Visa's practices reduced competition in the debit card market.

**3. Material Qualitative Risks and Offsets**
*   **Risk: Legal & Regulatory Scrutiny.** The reopened Pulse lawsuit (**2023-09-03**) directly challenges core debit market practices. This is an ongoing overhang with potential for enforced operational or business model changes. The broader regulatory environment for interchange fees and card networks remains a perennial risk.
    *   **Offset:** Visa's established, global scale and role as critical infrastructure for financial partners provide resilience. The company's business model, as described in its 10-K, inherently manages merchant discount rate and interchange complexity on behalf of clients.
*   **Risk: Intense Competition & Disruption.** The 10-K notes the payments industry faces "dynamic change" from "existing and emerging competitors," including fintechs, digital wallets, and blockchain/currencies. News articles (**2023-09-26**) also tie Visa's prospects to broader crypto/fintech volatility.
    *   **Offset:** Visa's strategy explicitly targets partnership with fintechs (e.g., Fast Track program) and pursues a "network of networks" approach. Its value-added services (tokenization, Cybersource, open banking via Tink) aim to entrench its position and diversify revenue streams beyond core switching.
*   **Risk: Macroeconomic Sensitivity.** CEO Al Kelly's comments referenced in the **2023-08-28** article acknowledge "short-term uncertainty," linking Visa's fortunes to consumer and business spending health.
    *   **Offset:** The secular shift from cash/physical payments to digital, as highlighted in company communications, provides a long-term tailwind that can moderate cyclical volatility.

**4. Concise Positive/Negative/Neutral Qualitative Assessment**
**Positive.** The supplied evidence paints a picture of a dominant franchise with multiple, durable growth levers (consumer payments, new flows, value-added services) that is actively executing on its strategic pillars. Recent narrative is overwhelmingly focused on growth catalysts, supported by article sentiment and cited financial performance. While the reopening of the Pulse lawsuit (**2023-09-03**) represents a material and unresolved legal risk, it is a known category of risk for the sector. The balance of evidence—market leadership, strategic adaptability, and a favorable long-term digital transition narrative—supports a positive qualitative stance at this point-in-time.

corrections.issue: Quant report states 'Latest operating margin is 0.645' but the raw evidence shows operating_margin=0.645 as a valuation ratio, not derived from the income statement. The income statement shows operating_income=5.02B on revenue=8.12B, which yields 61.8%, not 64.5%. The 0.645 figure appears to come from the valuation ratios panel and may reflect a different calculation methodology (e.g., TTM). The report should clarify the source and period of the operating margin figure to avoid ambiguity.
corrections.required_action: Note that the 0.645 operating margin is sourced from the valuation ratios panel (likely TTM-based) and may differ from the single-quarter calculation. No score change needed but flag for PM awareness.

corrections.issue: Quant report states 'Total assets grew from $82.90B (Q3 2021) to $89.23B (Q3 2023)' and 'Total equity increased from $37.59B to $38.98B'. These figures are correct per the raw balance sheet data. However, the report also states 'Total liabilities also grew, from $45.31B to $50.25B' — this is also correct. The report then notes 'liability growth has outpaced asset growth in recent quarters' which is supported: liabilities grew $4.94B (10.9%) vs assets grew $6.33B (7.6%) from Q3 2021 to Q3 2023, but more recently from Q2 2023 to Q3 2023, liabilities grew $2.06B while assets grew $2.48B, so the claim about 'recent quarters' is directionally correct for the full period but the per-quarter pace varies. Minor imprecision, no action required.
corrections.required_action: No action needed; claim is directionally supported.

corrections.issue: Qual report states 'Visa beat the Zacks Consensus Estimate for revenue ($8.1 billion, +12% YoY) and earnings per share (+9% YoY)' citing 2023-08-24 web context. The raw web context confirms revenue of $8,123 million advancing 12% YoY and bottom line improved 9% YoY. The quant report shows revenue growth of +11.7% YoY for Q3 2023. The slight discrepancy (12% vs 11.7%) is likely due to rounding in the news article. This is consistent and not a material conflict.
corrections.required_action: No action needed; minor rounding difference is expected between news summaries and precise calculations.
