ITEM 1.    BUSINESS
Overview
Salesforce is a global leader in customer relationship management ("CRM") technology that brings companies and their customers together. Founded in 1999, we enable companies of every size and industry to take advantage of powerful technologies to connect to their customers in a whole new way and help them transform their businesses around the customer in this digital-first world.
Our Customer 360 platform unites sales, service, marketing, commerce and IT teams by connecting customer data across systems, apps and devices to create a complete view of customers. With this single source of customer truth, teams can be more responsive, productive and efficient, deliver intelligent, personalized experiences across every channel and increase productivity. With Slack, we provide a digital headquarters where companies, employees, governments and stakeholders can collaborate to create success from anywhere.
Our Customer 360 service offerings are designed to be flexible, scalable and easy to use. They can generally be configured easily, deployed rapidly and integrated with other platforms and enterprise applications. We
…[truncated 20862 chars]…
consumer application companies that have entered the business software market; and
8
Table of Contents
- traditional platform development environment companies and cloud computing development platform companies who may develop toolsets and products that allow customers to build new apps that run on the customers' current infrastructure or as hosted services.
We believe more companies may become competitive threats due to the accelerated shift to cloud and hosted service offerings and customer experience management solutions. We also expect our competition to change and evolve as we expand into more markets, with new offerings.
Customers
We sell to businesses of all sizes and in almost every industry worldwide. The number of paying subscriptions at each of our customers ranges from one to hundreds of thousands. None of our customers accounted for more than five percent of our revenues in fiscal years 2023, 2022 or 2021. In addition, we do not have any material dependencies on any specific product, service or particular group or groups.
Customer Service and Support
We offer professional services to help customers achieve business results faster with Salesforce solutions. Our architects and innovation program teams act as advisors to plan and execute digital transformations for our customers. This includes implementation services for multi-cloud and complex deployments. We provide best-practices and AI-based recommendations and adoption programs globally. In addition, we provide advanced education, including in-person and online courses, to certify our customers and partners on architecting, administering, deploying and developing our service offerings.
Our global customer support group responds to both business and technical inquiries about the use of our products via the web, telephone, email, social networks and other channels. We provide standard customer support during regular business hours at no charge to customers who purchase any of our paying subscription editions. We also offer premier customer support that is either included in a premium success offering or sold for an additional fee, which can include services such as priority access to technical resources, developer support and system administration. In addition, we offer a premier priority support add-on that includes proactive monitoring, rapid incidence response and instruction from a dedicated support team knowledgeable about the customer's specific enterprise architecture.
Sales and Marketing
We sell our services primarily through our direct sales force, which comprises telephone sales personnel based in regional hubs, field sales personnel based in territories close to their customers and self-service offerings.
To a lesser extent, we also utilize a network of partners who refer sales leads to us and assist in selling to these prospects. This network includes global consulting firms, systems integrators and other partners. In return, we typically pay these partners a fee based on the first-year subscription revenue generated by the customers whom they refer. We continue to invest in developing additional distribution channels for our subscription services.
We use a variety of marketing programs across traditional and social channels to target our prospective and current customers, partners and developers. We focus our marketing activities in the cities and countries with the largest market opportunities. Our primary marketing activities include:
- multichannel marketing campaigns that span email, social media, the web, television and more, which align to a broader customer journey;
- in-person and virtual customer events of all sizes to create customer and prospect awareness, including proprietary events such as Dreamforce and our virtual Dreamforce to You, World Tours and other virtual events, as well as participation in trade shows and industry events;
- live events and original programming on our Salesforce+ streaming service, which includes discussions about the future of technology in the digital-first, work anywhere world and educational content to learn new skills and pursue new career opportunities;
- press and industry analyst relations to garner third-party validation and generate positive coverage for our company, brand, service offerings and value proposition;
- partner co-marketing activities with global and regional implementation partners;
- customer testimonials and our community of Trailblazers: individuals who drive innovation, grow their careers and transform their businesses using the Customer 360 platform;
- in-person and virtual technology event sponsorships; and
- event partnerships with high-profile global brands and organizations.
Intellectual Property
We rely on a combination of trademarks, copyrights, trade secrets and patents and contractual provisions, to protect our proprietary technology and our brands. We also enter into confidentiality and proprietary rights agreements with our employees, consultants and other third parties and control access to software, services, documentation and other proprietary information. We believe the duration of our patents is adequate relative to the expected lives of our service offerings. We also purchase or
9
Table of Contents
license technology that we incorporate into our products or services. At times, we make select intellectual property broadly available at no or low cost to achieve a strategic objective, such as promoting industry standards, advancing interoperability, supporting open source software or attracting and enabling our external development community. While it may be necessary in the future to seek or renew licenses relating to various aspects of our products and business methods, we believe, based upon past experience and industry practice, such licenses generally could be obtained on commercially reasonable terms.
Human Capital Management
Salesforce is committed to a core set of values: trust, customer success, innovation, equality and sustainability. These core values are the foundation of our company culture, which we believe is fundamental to, and a competitive advantage in, our approach to managing our workforce. We believe our company culture fosters open dialogue, collaboration, recognition and a sense of family, all of which allow us to attract and retain the best talent, which is critical for our continued success. For example, our sales, engineering and customer success teams are critical to our ability to grow, innovate and ensure the trust and customer success of our customers.
We believe our efforts in managing our workforce have been effective. Our focus on our workplace environment and a strong company culture has led to recognition across the globe, as evidenced by the following awards: Fortune World's Most Admired Companies (2022 and for the eighth year in a row), Fortune 100 Best Companies to Work For (2022 and for the 13th year in a row), Human Rights Campaign Best Places to Work for LGBTQ Equality (2022) and Glassdoor Employees' Choice Best Place to Work in Canada, France, Germany, the United Kingdom and the United States (2022).
As of January 31, 2023, we had 79,390 employees, of which approximately 52 percent were located in the United States and 48 percent were located internationally and approximately 36 percent identified as women, 63 percent identified as men and less than 0.2 percent identified as non-binary. This number includes those individuals impacted by our restructuring plan announced on January 4, 2023, which includes a reduction of our current workforce by approximately 10 percent. None of our employees in the United States are represented by a labor union. However, employees of certain foreign subsidiaries are represented by works councils.
We have continued to invest in equality, diversity and inclusion initiatives, development programs, employee engagement and ongoing communications and feedback. Some of our key human

# CRM Quantitative Assessment — Decision Date 2023-10-01

## 1. Revenue, Earnings, Margin & Balance-Sheet Trajectory

**Revenue** shows steady growth: Q2 2023-07-31 revenue of $8.60B, +4.3% QoQ from $8.25B (Q1 2023-04-30) and +11.4% YoY from $7.72B (Q2 2022-07-31). TTM revenue stands at $33.07B. The trajectory from Q3 2021 ($6.86B) to present is a consistent sequential climb across eight quarters.

**Earnings trajectory is erratic despite topline stability:**
- Q2 2023-07-31: EPS $1.28
- Q1 2023-04-30: EPS $0.20
- Q4 2023-01-31: EPS -$0.10
- Q3 2022-10-31: EPS $0.21
- Q2 2022-07-31: EPS $0.07

The latest EPS of $1.28 is a +540.0% QoQ increase and +1,728.6% YoY. However, the prior seven quarters averaged EPS of roughly $0.11, with three negative or near-zero prints. TTM net income is $1.58B on $33.07B revenue (TTM net margin ~4.8%).

**Margin volatility is pronounced:**
| Period | Gross Margin | Operating Margin | Net Margin |
|---|---|---|---|
| Q2 2023-07-31 | 75.5% | 17.2% | 14.8% |
| Q1 2023-04-30 | 74.2% | 5.0% | 2.4% |
| Q4 2023-01-31 | 74.9% | 4.3% | -1.2% |
| Q2 2022-07-31 | 72.4% | 2.5% | 0.9% |

Gross margin is consistently high (~72–76%). Operating margin swung from 2.5% (Q2 2022) to 17.2% (Q2 2023). The operating-income jump from $412M (Q1) to $1.48B (Q2) on only +$350M incremental revenue implies a major non-operating or one-time factor depressed Q1 (and prior quarters).

**Cash flow: Not available.** No free-cash-flow data is supplied for any period.

**Balance sheet:**
- Total equity: $58.08B (stable over period: range $57.05B–$60.10B)
- Total debt: $9.42B (slightly declining from $10.61B in Q3 2021)
- Debt-to-equity: 0.162 — low leverage
- Total assets: $92.45B (down from $98.85B at Q4 2023-01-31)

## 2. Earnings Quality & Balance-Sheet Resilience

**Earnings quality is suspect on two dimensions:**

*First*, the extreme EPS volatility relative to stable revenue growth is a red flag. Revenue grew +11.4% YoY, but EPS moved from $0.07 to $1.28 (+1,729%). The prior four-quarter EPS sum was approximately $0.23 ($0.21 + $0.07 + $0.03 + -$0.05), implying the recent quarter alone ($1.28) exceeds TTM of just two quarters ago by a factor of 5+. Operating income swung from $20M (Q1 2022) to $1.48B (Q2 2023) without proportionate revenue acceleration, suggesting restructuring charges, stock-based compensation, or other items that inflated the latest result or deflated prior results.

*Second*, gross margin improvement of +310bp YoY (72.4% → 75.5%) on $880M incremental revenue is notable but not fully explained by operating leverage alone, given the operating-income swing of $1.29B.

**Balance-sheet resilience is solid:** debt-to-equity of 0.162, total equity of $58.08B versus total debt of $9.42B. However, the declining asset base ($98.85B → $92.45B over three quarters, -$6.4B) warrants monitoring without cash-flow context.

**Cash generation is unobservable.** No free-cash-flow data is supplied. This is a critical gap: the high SBC inherent to enterprise software companies means GAAP earnings can substantially overstate distributable cash flow.

## 3. Valuation Versus Supplied History

**Current valuation (2023-09-29):**
| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| P/E (diluted) | 126.7x |
| P/S | 6.05x |
| P/B | 3.44x |
| Earnings yield | 0.8% |
| Net margin (trailing) | 4.8% |
| ROE | 2.7% |

**P/E trajectory over the supplied window:**
- 2022-10-03: 276.2x
- 2023-01-10: 530.4x
- 2023-05-19: ~1,000x (peak)
- 2023-09-29: 126.7x (current — within-window low)

The P/E compression from ~1,000x to 126.7x is driven almost entirely by the Q2 EPS surge, not by price decline. At 126.7x, the P/E is the lowest reading in the supplied 12-point history. The range was 126.5x–1,063.7x.

**P/B range:** 2.24x–3.88x; current 3.44x is in the upper half of the range.

**Key valuation concern:** The P/E of 126.7x is anchored to a single quarter's EPS ($1.28) that represents a 540% QoQ jump from an erratic history. If normalized TTM EPS is clo
…[truncated 596 chars]…
|---|
| ~Q (63d, ending 2023-09-29) | $211.26 | $202.78 | -4.0% |
| Prior Q | $196.60 | $211.26 | +7.5% |
| Prior Q | $128.47 | $196.60 | +53.0% |
| Prior Q | $150.17 | $128.47 | -14.5% |
| Annual (252d) | $150.17 | $202.78 | +35.0% |

The stock surged +53.0% in the December-to-March quarter (post-bear-market low) and continued +7.5% through June, but has stalled in Q3 (-4.0%). The annual +35.0% gain was front-loaded to early 2023 and has largely plateaued.

**Price-to-fundamental context:** The stock rose +35% over the year while TTM earnings moved from near-zero to $1.58B. However, the bulk of the price appreciation preceded the Q2 earnings inflection. The recent -4.0% quarterly drift occurred alongside the strongest EPS print in the history — a divergence that suggests the market has either priced in the improvement or remains skeptical of its durability.

## 5. Strongest Quantitative Upside & Downside

**Upside case (quantitative):**
- If Q2 operating margin of 17.2% is sustained at $8.6B+ quarterly revenue, annualized operating income would be ~$5.9B, versus TTM of $2.71B — a +118% increase. Applying a generous 50x P/E to such earnings would support meaningfully higher prices.
- P/B of 3.44x is below the window high of 3.88x; if equity growth continues alongside multiple expansion, upside exists.
- YoY revenue growth of 11.4% is accelerating (QoQ +4.3% implies an annualized ~18% run rate).

**Downside case (quantitative):**
- The Q2 EPS of $1.28 may be non-repeatable. If EPS reverts toward the prior four-quarter average (~$0.11), the stock at $202.78 would trade at an effective P/E of ~460x, extreme even for growth software.
- P/E of 126.7x is at the floor of the supplied range; any earnings miss would push the multiple back toward 300–500x+.
- The quarterly price return is already negative (-4.0%) despite the strongest EPS print, suggesting the rally may be exhausted.
- No cash-flow data means the $1.28 EPS may not convert to free cash flow; SBC could be substantial (unobservable but typical for CRM historically).
- Net margin has been sub-5% for most of the supplied history; the 14.8% Q2 print is an outlier.
- Total assets declined $6.4B in three quarters — cause unknown without cash-flow detail.

## 6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion

**Verdict: Fundamentally ambiguous, valuation demanding.**

The quantitative picture presents a sharp tension. Revenue growth of +11.4% YoY and gross margin of 75.5% are genuine positives, and the balance sheet is conservatively levered (D/E 0.162). However, the earnings trajectory is dominated by a single outlier quarter ($1.28 EPS) emerging from seven quarters of erratic, low profitability. The supplied P/E of 126.7x — while the lowest in the window — is mechanically inflated by one quarter and carries no cash-flow validation. TTM net margin of 4.8% and ROE of 2.7% reflect the longer-run profitability reality, not the Q2 anomaly.

The price has already delivered +35% annual return and is near the upper end of its 52-week range ($202.78 vs. high $238.22), yet has begun to stall (-4.0% QoQ) even as fundamentals improved. This divergence warrants caution: either the market is correctly pricing mean-reversion in margins, or the Q2 result is the beginning of an inflection. The supplied data cannot distinguish between these outcomes.

**Key risk:** If Q2's operating margin (17.2%) and EPS ($1.28) prove non-recurring, the stock at $202.78 is priced for sustained profitability it has not yet demonstrated across multiple quarters. **Key opportunity:** If the margin expansion is structural, revenue continues at ~11–18% growth, and the earnings base reprices from the Q1 2022-2023 trough, the valuation may rationalize from 126x toward 50–80x on materially higher earnings.

**Assessment: Neutral-to-cautious on risk/reward.** The lack of cash-flow evidence and the single-quarter dependence of the valuation make a confident directional call unsupported by the numbers alone.

### Qualitative Assessment: Salesforce (CRM)  
**Decision Date: 2023-10-01**  
**Evidence Window: 2023-08-12 to 2023-09-29**  

---

#### 1. Business/Franchise Read (from 10-K filed 2023-03-08)  
Salesforce is a global leader in customer relationship management (CRM) technology, offering an integrated Customer 360 platform that unifies sales, service, marketing, commerce, IT, and collaboration tools. The platform emphasizes a single source of customer truth, enabling personalized, data-driven experiences across channels. Key components include:  
- **Core Offerings**: Sales Cloud, Service Cloud, Marketing Cloud, Commerce Cloud, Tableau (analytics), MuleSoft (integration), and Slack (collaboration).  
- **Growth Strategy**: Focuses on cross-selling/up-selling to existing customers, expanding geographically, deepening industry-specific solutions (e.g., financial services, healthcare), and leveraging a partner ecosystem via AppExchange.  
- **Technology**: Cloud-native, multi-tenant architecture with ongoing investment in Hyperforce for global scalability and data residency control.  
- **Competitive Position**: Operates in a highly competitive, fragmented market with low barriers to entry; competes with traditional software vendors, cloud-native rivals, and free/low-cost alternatives.  

---

#### 2. Recent Developments (Dated)  
- **2023-08-31**: Salesforce raised its fiscal year 2024 adjusted operating margin forecast to ~30% (from 28%), with the CFO stating this is “a floor, not a ceiling.” Revenue and operating margin guidance were also lifted amid resilient demand and cost discipline.  
- **2023-09-22**: Salesforce announced the acquisition of AI customer service startup Airkit.ai to enhance conversational AI capabilities in Service Cloud, aiming for smarter, personalized customer engagement tools.  
- **2023-09-29**: Stock noted as down 8.3% since the last earnings report (30 days prior), reflecting market reactions despite the raised guidance.  
- **2023-09-29**: Global tech layoffs surpassed 238,000 workers year-to-date, indicating broader industry cost pressures.  

---

#### 3. Material Qualitative Risks and Offsets  
**Risks**:  
- **Legal/Reputational Exposure**: A former executive alleged (2023-08-12) that Salesforce misrepresented the real-time capabilities of its Genie Customer Data Cloud, potentially exposing the company to fraud claims and regulatory scrutiny. Ongoing litigation (e.g., *Bodine v. Salesforce*, 2023-09-18; *Yockey v. Salesforce*, 2023-08-25) may pose financial or reputational risks.  
- **Competition & Market Fragmentation**: The 10-K highlights intense competition from diverse players (e.g., enterprise software vendors, free-tier products, specialized niche providers), which could pressure pricing and market share.  
- **Integration Complexity**: Large acquisitions (Slack, Tableau, MuleSoft) and ongoing integration efforts may divert management focus or lead to operational inefficiencies.  
- **Macro Headwinds**: Widespread tech layoffs (2023-09-29) and economic uncertainty could moderate enterprise software spending.  

**Offsets**:  
- **Profitability Momentum**: Raised operating margin guidance (2023-08-31) signals effective cost management and improved profitability after activist pressure and restructuring.  
- **AI and Product Innovation**: Acquisitions like Airkit.ai (2023-09-22) and investments in Genie reflect strategic focus on AI-driven differentiation and real-time data capabilities.  
- **Diversified Customer Base**: No single customer exceeds 5% of revenue (10-K), reducing concentration risk.  
- **Values-Driven Culture**: Emphasis on trust, sustainability, and equality may support brand loyalty and talent retention.  

---

#### 4. Qualitative Assessment  
**Neutral**.  
Salesforce demonstrates a strong franchise with integrated platform capabilities and recent progress on profitability (raised guidance). However, the stock’s post-earnings decline and allegations of product misrepresentation introduce reputational and legal uncertainties. The company is actively investing in AI and cost discipline to sustain growth, but intense competition and macroeconomic headwinds present ongoing challenges. The balance of confirmed strategic moves and unresolved risks warrants a neutral near-term outlook.  

---

corrections.issue: Quant report states 'Operating income swung from $20M (Q1 2022) to $1.48B (Q2 2023)' — the raw pack shows Q1 2022 operating income was $20M (correct) but the report's narrative implies this was a single swing; the raw data shows operating income was actually negative (-$176M) in Q4 2022-01-31, which the report omits. This is a minor omission but the negative operating income quarter is more dramatic than the $20M starting point and strengthens the volatility argument.
corrections.required_action: No action needed; the volatility point is already well-supported, but the -176M quarter is a more striking anchor than $20M.

corrections.issue: Quant report calculates 'normalized P/E would be approximately 220x' using prior four-quarter average EPS of ~$0.23/quarter → $0.92 TTM. The raw pack shows the prior four quarters (Q1 2023 through Q2 2022) had EPS of $0.20, -$0.10, $0.21, $0.07 = $0.38 total, not $0.92. The report appears to use a different four-quarter grouping. The math $202.78/$0.92 ≈ 220x is internally consistent but the $0.92 figure is not clearly derived from the supplied data.
corrections.required_action: Clarify which four quarters are being averaged; the most recent prior four quarters (excluding Q2 2023) sum to $0.38 EPS, implying a normalized P/E closer to ~534x, which is even more extreme.

corrections.issue: Qual report states 'stock noted as down 8.3% since the last earnings report (30 days prior)' sourced to 2023-09-29 news. The quant report shows quarterly return of -4.0% (63 trading days). These are different timeframes and not contradictory, but the qual report's 8.3% figure is not directly verifiable from the supplied price data (which shows close of $202.78 on 2023-09-29 and $211.26 on 2023-06-30). The 8.3% decline likely refers to a post-earnings date not in the price series. This is acceptable sourcing from the news pack but worth noting the figure cannot be cross-checked.
corrections.required_action: No action required; the 8.3% figure is sourced from the news evidence and is not contradicted by the price data.

: Quant report states 'P/B range: 2.24x–3.88x' but the raw pack shows P/B range as 2.2–4.0. The upper bound should be 4.0, not 3.88. Minor rounding/reading discrepancy.

pm_decision_obj.executive_summary: Salesforce's Q2 2023 earnings inflection ($1.28 EPS, 17.2% operating margin) is a genuine positive, but the stock at 127x trailing P/E has already re-rated significantly (+35% annual return) and the durability of this profitability surge remains unproven across multiple quarters. The risk/reward is roughly balanced with a mild positive tilt from the raised margin guidance and AI investments, offset by valuation that prices in sustained execution.
pm_decision_obj.investment_thesis: Salesforce's Q2 2023 earnings inflection ($1.28 EPS, 17.2% operating margin) is a genuine positive, but the stock at 127x trailing P/E has already re-rated significantly (+35% annual return) and the durability of this profitability surge remains unproven across multiple quarters. The risk/reward is roughly balanced with a mild positive tilt from the raised margin guidance and AI investments, offset by valuation that prices in sustained execution.
key_factors: Q2 2023 EPS of $1.28 represents a 540% QoQ surge from an erratic history averaging ~$0.10/quarter over the prior four quarters, making the 127x P/E mechanically dependent on a single outlier quarter that may not be repeatable.

Operating margin guidance raised to ~30% floor (2023-08-31) with CFO calling it 'a floor, not a ceiling,' signaling structural cost discipline post-activist pressure and 10% workforce reduction in January 2023.
Revenue growth of +11.4% YoY to $8.60B (Q2 2023) with QoQ acceleration of +4.3% implies ~18% annualized run rate, while gross margin expanded 310bp YoY to 75.5%.

Item 1. Business
General Development of Business
Summary Description of Chevron
Chevron Corporation,* a Delaware corporation, manages its investments in subsidiaries and affiliates and provides administrative, financial, management and technology support to U.S. and international subsidiaries that engage in integrated energy and chemicals operations. Upstream operations consist primarily of exploring for, developing, producing and transporting crude oil and natural gas; processing, liquefaction, transportation and regasification associated with liquefied natural gas; transporting crude oil by major international oil export pipelines; transporting, storage and marketing of natural gas; and a gas-to-liquids plant. Downstream operations consist primarily of refining crude oil into petroleum products; marketing of crude oil, refined products, and lubricants; manufacturing and marketing of renewable fuels; transporting crude oil and refined products by pipeline, marine vessel, motor equipment and rail car; and manufacturing and marketing of commodity petrochemicals, plastics for industrial uses and fuel and lubricant additives.
A list of the company's significant subsidiaries is presented in Exhibit 21.1.
Overview of Petroleum Industry
Petroleum industry operations and profitability are influenced by many factors. Prices for crude oil, natural gas, liquefied natural gas, petroleum products and petrochemicals are generally determined by supply and demand. Production levels from the members of Organization of Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC), Russia and the United States are the major factors in determining worldwide supply. Demand for crude oil and its products and for natural gas is largely driven by the conditions of local, national and global economies, although weather patterns, the pace of energy transition and taxation relative to other energy sources also play a significant part. CVX.quantitative_report:
## CVX Quantitative Report — Decision Date 2023-10-01

### 1. Revenue, Earnings, Margin, Cash-Flow & Balance-Sheet Trajectory

**Revenue** peaked at Q2 2022 (68.76B) and has declined for four consecutive quarters to Q2 2023 (48.90B). QoQ: −3.7%; YoY: −28.9%. TTM revenue: 222.81B.

**Earnings** peaked in parallel: Q2 2022 EPS of 5.95 has fallen to Q2 2023 EPS of 3.20 (−46.2% YoY, −7.5% QoQ). TTM net income: 30.23B. TTM EPS (sum of last four reported quarters): 3.20 + 3.46 + 3.33 + 5.78 = 15.77.

**Margins** have compressed:
- Gross margin: Q2 2022 = 41.8% (28.76/68.76) → Q2 2023 = 40.7% (19.91/48.90) — relatively stable.
- Operating margin: Q2 2022 = 23.3% (16.00/68.76) → Q2 2023 = 16.0% (7.84/48.90) — significant contraction.
- Net margin: Q2 2022 = 17.0% (11.71/68.76) → Q2 2023 = 12.3% (6.01/48.90).

**Cash flow** data is entirely unavailable across all reported quarters. TTM free cash flow: n/a.

**Balance sheet** shows steady deleveraging and equity growth:
- Total equity: 136.72B (Q3 2021) → 159.30B (Q2 2023), +16.5%.
- Total liabilities: 103.23B (Q3 2021) → 92.48B (Q2 2023), −10.4%.
- Liabilities-to-equity ratio: 0.75x (Q3 2021) → 0.58x (Q2 2023).
- Total debt (last available, Q4 2021): 25.68B. No subsequent debt figures supplied.

### 2. Earnings Quality & Balance-Sheet Resilience

**Earnings quality** cannot be fully assessed: cash-flow data is entirely absent, so there is no independent verification that reported earnings convert to cash. The earnings stream is heavily cyclical and concentrated: Q3 2022 (5.78 EPS) alone accounts for 36.6% of TTM EPS (15.77). The operating-margin decline from 23.3% to 16.0% over four quarters signals deteriorating underlying profitability, not merely top-line contraction.

**Balance-sheet resilience** is a clear positive. Equity has grown by 22.58B since Q3 2021 while liabilities have declined by 10.75B. The leverage ratio at 0.58x is modest. However, without current debt data (last: 25.68B at Q4 2021), the precise debt picture is unknown.

### 3. Valuation versus Company's Own Supplied History

| Metric | Latest (2023-09-29) | 1-Year Range | Position in Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| P/E diluted | 10.46 | 8.0 – 12.2 | Upper half (68th percentile) |
| P/B | 1.99 | 1.8 – 2.3 | Mid-range (38th percentile) |
| P/S | 1.42 | — | No range supplied |
| Earnings yield | 9.6% | — | — |
| ROE | 19.0% | 0.19 – 0.224 (window) | Below recent peak |

The P/E of 10.46 sits in the upper half of its one-year range, not at a trough level. The low absolute P/E does not constitute cheapness on a standalone basis: the denominator (TTM EPS of ~15.77) is inflated by the peak Q3 2022 quarter. When that quarter rolls off, the forward P/E mechanically rises.

### 4. Price Level & Returns versus Fundamental Path

| Period | Price Change | Fundamental Direction |
|---|---|---|
| Q4 2022 → Q1 2023 | −8.2% (176.98 → 162.39) | Revenue −10.1% QoQ, EPS +3.9% QoQ |
| Q1 2023 → Q2 2023 | −3.1% (162.39 → 157.35) | Revenue −3.7% QoQ, EPS −7.5% QoQ |
| Q2 2023 → Q3 2023 | +7.2% (157.35 → 168.62) | **Not yet reported** |
| Annual (1 yr) | +15.7% (145.78 → 168.62) | Revenue −28.9% YoY, EPS −46.2% YoY |

The annual price return of +15.7% occurred alongside a −46.2% YoY EPS decline. The stock's 52-week high is 189.68; the current price of 168.62 is 11.1% below that high and 18.5% above the 52-week low of 142.27. Price has recovered in Q3 2023 while the most recently reported fundamental quarter (Q2 2023) showed continued deterioration.

### 5. Strongest Quantitative Upside & Downside

**Upside:**
- Gross margins remain resilient at ~40–42% despite a −28.9% revenue decline, suggesting pricing power or cost discipline is partially intact.
- Balance sheet is strong: 0.58x liabilities-to-equity, growing equity base (159.30B), and low leverage provide downside protection.
- If energy prices re-accelerate toward Q3 2022 levels, EPS could snap back toward 5.78–5.95 per quarter, implying annualized earnings power of ~$23 and a P/E below 7.3 at the current price.
- Earnings yield of 9.6% is attractive in absolute terms versus fixed-income alternatives.

**Downside:**
- TTM EPS of 15.77 is unsustainable: the peak Q3 2022 quarter (5.78 EPS) rolls off next. Replacing it with Q2 2023's run-rate (3.20 EPS) reduces TTM EPS to ~13.19, implying a forward P/E of **12.8** at the current price — near the top of the one-year range (12.2).
- Operating margin compression (23.3% → 16.0%) and net margin compression (17.0% → 12.3%) over four quarters signal structural or cyclical erosion, not stabilization.
- No free-cash-flow data exists to confirm that reported earnings are being converted to cash or that capital returns (dividends/buybacks) are funded sustainably.
- Price has risen +7.2% in the most recent quarter while the last reported fundamentals (Q2 2023) showed continued decline in revenue, margins, and earnings — a divergence.

### 6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion

CVX presents a **mixed risk/reward profile** anchored by a strong balance sheet but deteriorating earnings trajectory. The absolute P/E of 10.46 appears optically low, but it rests on a TTM earnings base that is artificially elevated by a single peak quarter (Q3 2022: 5.78 EPS, 36.6% of TTM). As that quarter rolls off, the effective P/E rises to ~12.8, moving toward the top of the one-year valuation range. Revenue has declined for four straight quarters (68.76B → 48.90B), and operating margins have compressed by 730 bps.

The balance sheet (159.30B equity, 0.58x leverage) provides resilience, and the 9.6% earnings yield is nominally attractive. However, the absence of any cash-flow data means earnings quality is unverifiable. Price has already recovered +7.2% in Q3 2023 despite no evidence of fundamental improvement in the last reported quarter, narrowing the prospective upside.

**Quantitative lean: cautious.** The combination of (a) peak-earnings dependency in the TTM denominator, (b) confirmed multi-quarter revenue and margin decline, (c) price appreciation ahead of reported fundamentals, and (d) absent cash-flow verification tilts the near-term risk/reward to the downside, with the balance sheet acting as a partial floor.
CVX.qualitative_report:
**Qualitative Equity Research Report: Chevron Corporation (CVX)**
**Date of Analysis:** 2023-10-01
**Evidence Cut-off:** 2023-09-29

**1. Business/Franchise Read from Supplied Narrative**
Per the 2022 10-K (filing_date=2023-02-23), Chevron is a fully integrated global energy company. Its operations are split into Upstream (exploring for, developing, producing, and transporting crude oil, natural gas, and LNG) and Downstream (refining, marketing, and transporting crude oil and refined products, as well as manufacturing petrochemicals and renewable fuels). The company's stated strategy is to deliver "higher returns, lower carbon" by growing traditional oil and gas while lowering operational carbon intensity and developing new lower-carbon businesses (e.g., renewable fuels, hydrogen, carbon capture).

**2. Recent Developments (Dated from Supplied Text)**
*   **Shareholder Returns & Investment Narrative (2023-09-29):** Multiple news articles consistently position Chevron as a premier "dividend stock" for conservative and income-focused investors, frequently mentioned alongside other large-cap payers. It is highlighted as a core holding in Warren Buffett's portfolio.
*   **Legal & Regulatory Risk Materialization (2023-09-18):** The state of California sued major oil companies, including Chevron, "accusing them of playing down the risks posed by fossil fuels." This represents a direct, high-profile regulatory and legal challenge.
*   **Production Guidance (2023-02-23):** In its 10-K, Chevron estimated its 2023 average worldwide oil-equivalent production to be "flat to up three percent compared to 2022," assuming an $80/barrel Brent crude price.
*   **Capital Allocation (2023-08-22):** The company elected to terminate an exchange offer and consent solicitation, as noted in a web context snippet.

**3. Material Qualitative Risks and Offsets**
*   **Risks:**
    *   **Regulatory & Litigation Escalation (Confirmed, 2023-09-18):** The California lawsuit signifies heightened legal and political pressure on the oil and gas industry, which could lead to increased costs, operational restrictions, or reputational damage.
    *   **Energy Transition & Demand Risk (Confirmed, 2023-02-23):** The 10-K explicitly states that "laws and governmental policies, particularly in the areas of taxation, energy and the environment, affect where and how companies invest, conduct their operations... and, in some cases, limit their profits directly." The pace of the energy transition remains a fundamental demand-side risk.
    *   **Operational & Project Execution (Confirmed, 2023-08-22):** The company's own filing (referenced in web context) notes risks including "potential failure to achieve expected net production... potential delays in the development, construction or start-up of planned projects."
*   **Offsets:**
    *   **Shareholder Return Focus (Confirmed, 2023-09-29):** The strong market narrative as a leading dividend payer supports its capital return philosophy, a key tenet for attracting a certain investor base.
    *   **Lower-Carbon Strategic Positioning (Confirmed, 2023-02-23):** The articulated strategy to grow lower-carbon businesses (renewable fuels, hydrogen, carbon capture) and reduce operational carbon intensity is a stated counter-strategy to energy transition risks. A web context snippet (2023-09-26) notes a specific target to reduce carbon intensity by 5% by 2028.
    *   **Scale and Integration (Confirmed, 2023-02-23):** The fully integrated model across upstream and downstream, with global asset diversification, provides a structural hedge against volatility in any single segment or region.

**4. Qualitative Assessment**
**Neutral with Negative Bias.**
The core franchise as a diversified, integrated energy major remains intact, supported by a strong narrative around shareholder returns. However, recent developments highlight a clear escalation in material regulatory and legal risks (California lawsuit) that directly threaten the industry's social license and potential profitability. While the company is strategically pivoting toward lower-carbon energy, the timeline and financial scale of this transition relative to legacy business cash flows introduce uncertainty. The assessment balances the stable dividend/income story against the confirmed emergence of significant political and legal headwinds.

corrections.issue: Quant report states 'ROE 0.19 – 0.224 (window)' for the 1-year range, but the raw valuation data shows ROE values ranging from 0.189 to 0.224 across the sparse history points. The lower bound should be 0.189, not 0.19. This is a minor rounding discrepancy but worth noting for precision.
corrections.required_action: No action needed; the 0.19 vs 0.189 difference is within rounding tolerance and does not change the directional conclusion.

corrections.issue: The qual report states Chevron's carbon intensity reduction target as '5% by 2028' citing a web context snippet dated 2023-09-26. The raw web context confirms this: 'Chevron aims to reduce its PCI by 5% by 2028 compared to 2016.' However, the same source also states 'Chevron's 1.5°C pathway requires a reduction of its PCI by nearly 5% annually between 2022 and 2027,' which implies the company's actual target is far less ambitious than what a 1.5°C-aligned pathway would require. The qual report omits this gap, which is material to assessing the energy-transition offset claim.
corrections.required_action: PM should note that Chevron's stated 5% cumulative target by 2028 is materially weaker than the ~5% annual reduction implied by a 1.5°C pathway, weakening the qualitative offset from lower-carbon positioning.

corrections.issue: The quant report's forward P/E calculation of ~12.8 assumes Q3 2022 (5.78 EPS) is replaced by Q2 2023 (3.20 EPS) in the TTM. The arithmetic is correct: (3.20 + 3.46 + 3.33 + 3.20) = 13.19, and 168.62 / 13.19 ≈ 12.78. However, the report does not note that Q3 2023 has not yet been reported and actual Q3 2023 earnings could differ materially from Q2 2023's run-rate. The forward P/E of 12.8 is a mechanical floor estimate, not a forecast, and should be read as such.
corrections.required_action: No correction needed; the report appropriately frames this as a mechanical roll-off scenario. PM should treat the 12.8 forward P/E as a bearish floor, not a point estimate.

unsupported_claims: : The qual report's characterization of Chevron as a 'premier dividend stock' and 'core holding in Warren Buffett's portfolio' is supported by the news articles in the raw evidence (multiple articles from 2023-09-29 reference this). No unsupported claim here.
verified_points

pm_decision_obj.executive_summary: Chevron's strong balance sheet (0.58x leverage, $159.3B equity) and 9.6% earnings yield provide a floor, but deteriorating fundamentals—four consecutive quarters of revenue decline, 730bps operating margin compression, and a TTM EPS inflated by a peak quarter rolling off—offset the valuation case. The stock's +7.2% Q3 recovery ahead of reported fundamental improvement narrows near-term upside, leaving a roughly balanced risk/reward.
pm_decision_obj.investment_thesis: Chevron's strong balance sheet (0.58x leverage, $159.3B equity) and 9.6% earnings yield provide a floor, but deteriorating fundamentals—four consecutive quarters of revenue decline, 730bps operating margin compression, and a TTM EPS inflated by a peak quarter rolling off—offset the valuation case. The stock's +7.2% Q3 recovery ahead of reported fundamental improvement narrows near-term upside, leaving a roughly balanced risk/reward.
key_factors: TTM EPS of 15.77 is artificially elevated by Q3 2022's 5.78 EPS (36.6% of TTM); as that quarter rolls off, forward P/E rises to ~12.8x, near the top of the 1-year range (12.2x), eliminating the apparent cheapness.

Revenue declined for four straight quarters (68.76B → 48.90B, -28.9% YoY) and operating margin compressed from 23.3% to 16.0%, signaling sustained earnings erosion beyond commodity price normalization.
Balance sheet resilience is a clear positive: equity grew to 159.3B, liabilities fell to 92.5B, and the 0.58x leverage ratio provides downside protection and supports continued capital returns.

Price has already recovered +7.2% in Q3 2023 (to 168.62) while the last reported quarter (Q2 2023) showed continued revenue, margin, and earnings decline—a divergence that limits near-term upside without a fundamental catalyst.

Item 1. Business
Overview
Walgreens Boots Alliance, Inc., a Delaware corporation ("Walgreens Boots Alliance" or the "Company"), is an integrated healthcare, pharmacy and retail leader serving millions of customers and patients every day, with a 170-year heritage of caring for communities. The Company is proud of its contributions to healthy communities, a healthy planet, an inclusive workplace and a sustainable marketplace. Walgreens Boots Alliance is a participant of the United Nations Global Compact and adheres to its principles-based approach to responsible business. The Company has been recognized for its commitment to operating sustainably; it is an index component of the Dow Jones Sustainability Indices ("DJSI") and was named to the 100 Best Corporate Citizens of 2022.
A trusted, global innovator in retail pharmacy with approximately 13,000 locations across the U.S., Europe and Latin America, Walgreens Boots Alliance plays a critical role in the healthcare ecosystem. The Company is reimagining local healthcare and well-being for all as part of its purpose – to create more joyful lives through better health. Through dispensing medicines, improving access to a wide range of health services, providing high quality health and beauty products and offering anytime, anywhere convenience acro
…[truncated 20706 chars]…
nts of the segment's sales are Pharmacy (the sale of prescription drugs and provision of pharmacy-related services) and Retail (the sale of healthcare and retail products including non-prescription drugs, health and wellness, beauty and personal care, and consumables and general merchandise). The segment's sales are subject to the influence of seasonality, particularly the cough, cold and flu seasons and winter holiday. This seasonality also can affect the segment's proportion of sales between Retail and Pharmacy during certain periods. The components of the segment's fiscal year sales were as follows:
|  | Fiscal 2022 | Fiscal 2022 | Fiscal 2021 | Fiscal 2021 | Fiscal 2020 | Fiscal 2020 |
| Pharmacy | 74 | 74% | 76 | 76% | 75 | 75% |
| Retail | 26 | 26% | 24 | 24% | 25 | 25% |
| Total | 100 | 100% | 100 | 100% | 100 | 100% |
The Company filled 819.6 million prescriptions (including vaccinations) in the segment in fiscal 2022. Adjusted to 30-day equivalents, prescriptions filled were 1.2 billion in fiscal 2022. The Company fills prescriptions under Medicare, Medicaid and other publicly financed or sponsored health benefit and prescription drug plans and programs, including the federal 340B drug pricing program. Sales where reimbursement is received from managed care organizations, governmental agencies, PBM companies and private insurance were approximately 97% of the segment's fiscal 2022 Pharmacy sales.
The Company fills prescriptions for many state Medicaid public assistance programs. Sales from all such Medicaid plans were approximately 5% of the segment's fiscal 2022 sales. Sales from Medicare Part D plans were approximately 22% of the segment's fiscal 2022 sales.
The Company's myWalgreens Credit Card program features the myWalgreens Mastercard and the myWalgreens Credit Card. These cards are the first ever of their kind to reward more personalized wellbeing choices and offer industry-leading rewards at Walgreens locations, Walgreens.com, Duane Reade stores, via the Walgreens mobile app, and wherever Mastercard is accepted.
AmerisourceBergen supplies and distributes a significant amount of generic and branded pharmaceutical products to the segment's pharmacies. The Company purchases its non-pharmaceutical merchandise from numerous manufacturers and wholesalers.
The segment's sales, gross profit margin and gross profit are impacted by, among other things, both the percentage of prescriptions filled that are generic and the rate at which new generic drugs are introduced to the market. Because any number of factors outside of the Company's control can affect timing for a generic conversion, the Company faces substantial uncertainty in predicting when such conversions will occur and what effect they will have on particular future periods.
WBA Fiscal 2022 Form 10-K 5
Table of Contents
The current environment of the Company's pharmacy business also includes ongoing reimbursement pressure, a shift in pharmacy mix towards 90-day at retail (one prescription that is the equivalent of three 30-day prescriptions), an increased volume of Medicare Part D prescriptions and increased consumer use of prescription discount cards. Further consolidation among generic manufacturers coupled with changes in the number of major brand name drugs anticipated to undergo a conversion from branded to generic status may also result in gross margin pressures within the industry.
The Company continuously faces reimbursement pressure from PBM companies, government, health maintenance organizations, managed care organizations and other commercial third-party payers. Agreements with these payers are regularly subject to expiration, termination or renegotiation. In addition, plan changes with rate adjustments often occur in January and the Company's reimbursement arrangements may provide for rate adjustments at prescribed intervals during their term. The Company experienced lower reimbursement rates in fiscal 2022 as compared to the same period in the prior year. The Company expects these pressures to continue.
The Company has also worked to develop and expand its relationships with commercial third-party payers to enable new and/or improved market access via participation in pharmacy provider networks they offer. The prescription volume impact of new agreements and relationships typically is incremental over time.
The Company's 90-day at retail prescription drug offering is typically at a lower margin than comparable 30-day prescriptions, but provides the Company with the opportunity to increase business with patients with chronic prescription needs while offering increased convenience, helping facilitate improved prescription adherence and resulting in a lower cost to fill the 90-day prescription. Similarly, the specialty prescription business, which generates higher sales dollars per prescription, may result in gross margin pressures within the industry, as compared to generic prescription drugs. The segment's performance is also impacted by the current environment, including the uncertainty as a result of COVID-19. For more information, see Risk factors in Item 1A.
International
The International segment consists of pharmacy-led health and beauty retail businesses outside the U.S. and the pharmaceutical wholesaling and distribution business in Germany.
Pharmacy-led health and beauty retail businesses include Boots branded stores in the United Kingdom ("UK"), the Republic of Ireland and Thailand, the Benavides brand in Mexico and the Ahumada brand in Chile. Sales for these businesses are principally derived from the sale of prescription drugs and health and wellness, beauty, personal care and other consumer products. The Company operated 3,989 retail stores in the segment as of August 31, 2022 (see properties in Part I, Item 2 for information regarding geographic coverage) and has grown its omni-channel platform, including its online presence, in recent years. In the UK, the Company is a market leader and its retail stores are conveniently located with pharmacists well placed to provide a significant role in the provision of healthcare services, working closely with other primary healthcare providers in the communities the Company serves.
The Boots omni-channel offering is differentiated from that of competitors due to the product brands the Company owns, such as No7, Liz Earle, Soap & Glory, Botanics, Sleek MakeUp, Boots Pharmaceuticals and 'only at Boots' exclusive products, together with its long established reputation for trust and customer care. The Company's brands portfolio is enhanced by its in-house product research and development capabilities. The Company has introduced new beauty brands and beauty halls in key locations. Certain of the product brands of the Company are also sold by third-party retailers.
The Company's retail store networks are typically complemented by online platforms. In the UK, through the boots.com website and integrated mobile application, the 'order and collect' service normally allows customers to order from a range of over 37,000 products by 5:00 p.m. and collect the following day from approximately 98% of the UK's retail stores.
The Boots Advantage Card loyalty program, where customers earn points on purchases for redemption at a later date, continues to be a key element of the Boots offering. As of August 31, 2022, the number of active Boots Advantage Card members (members who have used their card in the last six months) totaled approximately 13 million.
In addition, Boots in the UK is one of the leaders in the optical market with 543 practices, of which 160 operated on a franchise basis as of August 31, 2022. Approximately 30% of these optical practices are located in Boots stores with the balance being standalone optical practices.

## WBA Quantitative Report — As of 2023-09-29 (PIT)

---

### 1. Revenue, Earnings, Margin, and Balance-Sheet Trajectory

**Revenue:** Quarterly revenue bottomed at $32.45B (Q4 2022-08-31) and has recovered to $35.41B (Q3 2023-05-31), a YoY increase of +8.6%. TTM revenue stands at $136.11B. The revenue trend is modestly positive.

**Earnings:** EPS trajectory is severely impaired. After oscillating around positive values ($0.72–$4.13) through FY2021, EPS plunged to **−$4.31** in Q1 FY2023 (2022-11-30), followed by **−$0.48**, then **+$0.81**, and most recently only **+$0.14** in Q3 2023. TTM net income is **−$3.73B** and TTM operating income is **−$6.85B**. The latest quarter's net income is **−$48M** despite $35.41B in revenue.

**Margins:** Gross margin has compressed from **22.8%** (Q2 2022-02-28) to **18.6%** (Q3 2023-05-31). Operating margin, even excluding the anomalous Q1 FY2023 charge (−18.4%), has deteriorated: Q2 2022 +3.7% → Q3 2022 −1.0% → Q4 2022 −1.3% → Q2 2023 +0.6% → Q3 2023 −1.3%. The current TTM operating margin is approximately **−5.0%**. Net margin (TTM) is **−2.8%**.

**Cash Flow:** No cash-flow statement data is available across all eight quarters.

**Balance Sheet:** Total equity has fluctuated between $23.82B (2021-08-31) and $30.87B (2022-02-28), currently at **$29.36B**. Total liabilities stand at **$69.09B** on **$98.61B** of assets. Total debt was **$11.71B** as of 2022-08-31 (latest available), implying a debt-to-equity ratio of approximately **0.40x**. The equity base absorbed the large Q1 FY2023 loss ($24.58B at 2022-11-30) but partially recovered by 2023-02-28 ($29.44B).

---

### 2. Earnings Quality and Balance-Sheet Resilience

**Earnings Quality:** Poor. The TTM net income is **−$3.73B** driven heavily by the **−$3.82B** single-quarter loss in Q1 FY2023 (2022-11-30), which also generated **−$6.15B** in operating losses. Even abstracting from that quarter, the remaining three quarters produce a combined operating income of roughly **−$700M** on ~$103B in revenue—indicating the core business is barely breaking even or losing money at the operating level. The most recent quarter (Q3 2023) shows **−$477M** operating income on $35.41B revenue, a recurring operating loss pattern. Gross margins are declining sequentially across multiple quarters.

**Balance-Sheet Resilience:** Moderate. Debt-to-equity of ~0.40x is manageable. Total equity of $29.36B provides a buffer, and the book value is substantial relative to the market cap (P/B = 0.654 implies market cap ≈ $19.2B). However, the absence of cash-flow data means liquidity and debt-servicing capacity cannot be independently verified. The balance sheet absorbed a $5B+ quarterly equity hit and recovered, suggesting some resilience, but persistent operating losses would erode equity over time.

---

### 3. Valuation Versus Supplied History

| Metric | Current (2023-09-29) | Historical Range (12 pts) |
|---|---|---|
| P/B | **0.654** | 0.924 (Oct 2022) → 1.304 (Feb 2023) → 0.654 |
| P/S | **0.141** | Not tracked historically in dataset |
| Net Margin | **−2.8%** | +3.9% (Oct 2022) → −2.5% (Jan 2023) → −2.8% |
| ROE | **−13%** | +17% (Oct 2022) → −13.4% (Jan 2023) → −13% |
| P/E (diluted) | **Not meaningful** (negative TTM earnings) | 5.4–8.1 (Oct–Dec 2022, last valid readings) |

P/B has collapsed from **1.304** to **0.654**, a decline of **50%** in seven months, placing it at the floor of the observed range (0.6). ROE swung from **+17%** to **−13%** as earnings flipped negative. The P/E range of **5.1–8.3** is stale and no longer applicable given TTM losses. At a P/B of 0.654, the stock trades at a **35% discount to book value**, but this is conditional on the earnings trajectory: ROE is −13% and net margins are negative, so the discount reflects impaired profitability rather than representing clear value. The valuation discount is earned by the deterioration in returns.

---

### 4. Price Level and Return Context

**Current price:** **$22.24** (2023-09-29), within **8%** of the 52-week low of **$20.58** and **47%** below the 52-week high of **$42.29**.

**Quarterly returns:**
- Q3 2023 (Jun→Sep): **−21.9%**
- Q2 2023 (Mar→Jun): **−17.7%**
- Q1 2023 (Dec→Mar): **−7.8%**
- Q4 2022 (Sep→Dec): **+13.2%**

**Annual return:** **−33.0%**

The price has declined in **three consecutive quarters** totaling approximately **−41%** from the Q4 2022 peak. The sell-off accelerates each quarter, consistent with the fundamental deterioration: margin compression, recurring operating losses, and the large one-time charge. The stock is now priced at a deep P/B discount that coincides with a confirmed negative earnings trajectory, not a temporary dislocation.

---

### 5. Strongest Quantitative Upside and Downside

**Upside:**
- P/B of **0.654** is at the lowest point in the 12-point history (range floor 0.6). If operating margins can revert toward the +3.7% level seen in Q2 FY2022, ROE would normalize and the book-value discount could narrow significantly. Revenue is growing (+8.6% YoY), and the equity base remains intact at $29.36B.
- Debt-to-equity of ~0.40x provides financial flexibility absent a liquidity crisis.

**Downside:**
- Operating margins have been negative in **five of the last six reported quarters** (excluding Q2 2023's +0.6%). Gross margins are compressing (22.8% → 18.6%). If the operating loss trend persists, the $29.36B equity base will erode, making the current P/B less of a discount and more of a fair reflection of impaired assets.
- No cash-flow data exists to confirm whether operating losses translate into cash burn or whether working capital and capex are manageable.
- The price trajectory shows accelerating quarterly declines (−7.8% → −17.7% → −21.9%), and the stock is near its 52-week low with no fundamental inflection visible in the latest quarter's numbers.

---

### 6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion

WBA presents a **low-quality value trap profile** as of the decision date. The P/B of **0.654** and P/S of **0.141** appear optically cheap, but they are justified by a TTM net loss of **−$3.73B**, TTM operating loss of **−$6.85B**, negative ROE of **−13%**, and deteriorating gross margins (22.8% → 18.6%). The most recent quarter (Q3 2023) shows no earnings inflection—EPS of just **$0.14** and an operating loss of **$477M** on $35.41B revenue. Balance-sheet equity of **$29.36B** provides a book-value backstop, and leverage is moderate at ~0.40x D/E, but the absence of cash-flow data prevents confirmation of cash generation. The stock has fallen **−33% annually** and **−21.9% in the latest quarter** with accelerating momentum to the downside, closing within **8%** of its 52-week low.

The fundamental path is one of margin compression and operating losses; the price decline is tracking that deterioration rather than overshooting it. Until operating margins return to positive territory on a sustained basis, the valuation discount reflects impaired earnings power rather than a mispricing. **Risk/reward is unfavorable for a fundamental long** absent evidence of an earnings recovery catalyst in the supplied numbers.
WBA.qualitative_report:
**Qualitative Assessment Report: Walgreens Boots Alliance (WBA)**
**Decision Date: 2023-10-01**

**1. Business/Franchise Read**
Based on the 2022 10-K (filing date 2022-10-13), Walgreens Boots Alliance is an integrated healthcare, pharmacy, and retail leader operating approximately 13,000 locations globally. The business is structured into three segments: U.S. Retail Pharmacy (sales of $109.1B in FY2022), International ($21.8B), and U.S. Healthcare ($1.8B). The core franchise is a pharmacy-led retail network serving as a critical healthcare access point, with ~78% of the U.S. population living within five miles of a store. A key strategic pivot is the buildout of a U.S. Healthcare segment through majority acquisitions in VillageMD (primary care), Shields (hospital/health system pharmacy), and CareCentrix (post-acute/home care), aiming to create an integrated, value-based care platform. The company also holds a significant equity method investment in AmerisourceBergen.

**2. Recent Developments (Dated)**
*   **Corporate Action / Leadership (Sep 29, 2023):** WBA announced the appointment of Tim Wentworth, former CEO of Express Scripts, as its new CEO, effective immediately. The announcement noted this came after the company expected FY23 adjusted EPS to be "at or near the low end" of its previously stated range.
*   **Strategic Partnership (Sep 12, 2023):** Walgreens partnered with startup Pearl Health to accelerate its push into value-based care. The move is part of its strategy to "move more aggressively into healthcare," according to its Chief Growth Officer.
*   **Competitive Environment (Sep 30, 2023):** Costco began offering members access to low-cost virtual primary care visits ($29) through a partnership with Sesame, representing a new competitive entry in the healthcare services space.
*   **Financial Guidance (Referenced Sep 29, 2023):** Prior to the CEO announcement, the company communicated that it expected FY23 adjusted EPS to be at the low end of its previously guided range.

**3. Material Qualitative Risks and Offsets**
*   **Risks:**
    *   **Regulatory & Legal (Sep 21 & Aug 15, 2023):** The company faces ongoing legal challenges, including a lawsuit following an FDA warning over eye drop products and a decision allowing a U.S. and Virginia Medicaid fraud lawsuit to proceed. These create potential financial liabilities and reputational harm.
    *   **Leadership Transition (Sep 29, 2023):** The CEO change introduces execution risk during a critical strategic transformation toward healthcare services.
    *   **Operational & Competitive (Sep 28, 2023):** The stock price reflects significant market concern, trading at decade lows. The core pharmacy business faces sustained reimbursement pressure and competition from new healthcare entrants (e.g., Costco).
    *   **Strategic Execution:** The large-scale pivot into value-based care via acquisitions (VillageMD, Shields, CareCentrix) carries integration risk and requires significant capital with uncertain near-term returns, as evidenced by recent guidance.
*   **Offsets:**
    *   **Experienced New Leadership (Sep 29, 2023):** The incoming CEO's background in healthcare benefits management (Express Scripts) is viewed as a potential long-term catalyst to navigate the healthcare strategy.
    *   **Scale and Network Advantage:** The company's vast retail pharmacy footprint (from 10-K) provides a foundational asset for distributing healthcare services.
    *   **Strategic Partnerships (Sep 12, 2023):** Alliances like with Pearl Health are intended to accelerate the healthcare transition without sole reliance on internal development.

**4. Qualitative Assessment: Neutral**
The WBA franchise possesses clear scale and a defined strategic direction toward healthcare services, supported by significant recent investments and partnerships. However, as of the decision date, the narrative is dominated by material headwinds: a deteriorating near-term earnings outlook, a complex CEO transition, persistent competitive and reimbursement pressures in the core business, and unresolved legal/regulatory issues. The transformative healthcare strategy is in its early stages and faces execution risk. The positive potential from new leadership and the healthcare network buildout is currently counterbalanced by these confirmed operational and financial challenges, leading to a neutral qualitative stance.

However, the qual report does not mention the significant goodwill and intangible asset risks from the VillageMD ($5.2B), Shields ($969M + $1.37B), and CareCentrix ($332M + $392B) acquisitions detailed in the 10-K, which are material to understanding balance-sheet quality and potential impairment risk. → Note that the 10-K details substantial healthcare segment acquisitions (VillageMD ~$5.2B, Shields ~$2.3B, CareCentrix ~$724M) that create goodwill/intangible asset concentration risk relevant to the $29.36B equity base. This is omitted context the PM should weigh.
- [C3] The quant report correctly notes absence of cash-flow data and flags this as a limitation. The qual report does not address cash-flow or liquidity risk at all, despite the company's negative operating income trajectory and large acquisition spending documented in the 10-K. This is a gap in the qualitative risk assessment. → Acknowledge that cash-flow/liquidity risk is unaddressed in the qualitative report and should be flagged as a material blind spot given the operating losses and acquisition-driven capital deployment.

**Unsupported claims:**
- The quant report claims 'five of the last six reported quarters' had negative operating margins. The raw income statement shows operating income of: +358M (2021-08-31), +1.28B (2021-11-30), +1.25B (2022-02-28), -320M (2022-05-31), -415M (2022-08-31), -6.15B (2022-11-30), +197M (2023-02-28), -477M (2023-05-31). Of the most recent six quarters, four are negative and two are positive — not five of six.

pm_decision_obj.executive_summary: WBA is a structurally deteriorating business with TTM net losses of $3.73B, negative operating margins persisting across four of the last six quarters, and no earnings inflection visible in the most recent quarter (Q3 2023 EPS of just $0.14). The P/B of 0.654 reflects justified fundamental impairment rather than a value opportunity, and the accelerating price decline (three consecutive quarters totaling -41%) signals the market is correctly repricing the earnings trajectory.
pm_decision_obj.investment_thesis: WBA is a structurally deteriorating business with TTM net losses of $3.73B, negative operating margins persisting across four of the last six quarters, and no earnings inflection visible in the most recent quarter (Q3 2023 EPS of just $0.14). The P/B of 0.654 reflects justified fundamental impairment rather than a value opportunity, and the accelerating price decline (three consecutive quarters totaling -41%) signals the market is correctly repricing the earnings trajectory.
key_factors: TTM net loss of -$3.73B with TTM operating income of -$6.85B and operating margin of approximately -5.0%, confirming deep fundamental deterioration with no earnings recovery in the most recent quarter (Q3 2023 operating loss of -$477M on $35.41B revenue)

P/B has collapsed from 1.304 to 0.654 (-50% in seven months) with ROE at -13%, indicating the valuation discount is earned by impaired profitability rather than representing a mispricing; the stock is within 8% of its 52-week low of $20.58

Gross margin compression from 22.8% (Q2 2022) to 18.6% (Q3 2023) across multiple quarters, with negative operating income in four of the last six reported quarters, signaling persistent structural headwinds in the core pharmacy business

New CEO appointment (Sep 29, 2023) introduces execution risk during a critical healthcare transformation, while ongoing legal/regulatory challenges (FDA eye drop warning, Medicaid fraud lawsuit) and new competitive entrants (Costco virtual care at $29) add further uncertainty to an already stressed business model

ing, and inclusive work environment that enables our global employees to create products and services that further our mission.
8
Item 1
As of June 30, 2023, we employed approximately 221,000 people on a full-time basis, 120,000 in the U.S. and 101,000 internationally. Of the total employed people, 89,000 were in operations, including manufacturing, distribution, product support, and consulting services; 72,000 were in product research and development; 45,000 were in sales and marketing; and 15,000 were in general and administration. Certain employees are subject to collective bargaining agreements.
Our Culture
Microsoft's culture is grounded in growth mindset. This means everyone is on a continuous journey to learn and grow, operating as one company instead of multiple siloed businesses.
Our employee listening systems enable us to gather feedback directly from our workforce to inform our programs and employee needs globally. Employees participate in our Employee Signals surveys, which cover a variety of topics such as thriving, inclusion, team culture, wellbeing, and learning and development. We also collect Daily Signals employee survey responses, giving us real-time insights into ways we can support our employees. In addition to Employee Signals and Daily Signals surveys, we gain insights through onboarding, exit surveys, internal Viva Engage channels, employee Q&A sessions, and our internal AskHR Service support.
Diversity and inclusion are core to our business model, and we hold ourselves accountable for driving global systemic change in our workforce and creating an inclusive work environment. We support multiple highly active Employee Resource Groups for women, families, racial and ethnic minorities, military, people with disabilities, and employees who identify as LGBTQIA+, where employees can go for support, networking, and community-building. As described in our 2022 Proxy Statement, annual performance and compensation reviews of our senior leadership team include an evaluation of their contributions to employee culture and diversity. To ensure accountability over time, we publicly disclose our progress on a multitude of workforce metrics including:
- Detailed breakdowns of gender, racial, and ethnic minority representation in our employee population, with data by job types, levels, and segments of our business.
- Our EEO-1 report (equal employment opportunity).
- Disability representation.
- Pay equity (see details below).
Total Rewards and Pay Equity
We develop dynamic, sustainable, market-driven, and strategic programs with the goal of providing a highly differentiated portfolio to attract, reward, and retain top talent and enable our employees to thrive. These programs reinforce our culture and values such as collaboration and growth mindset. Managers evaluate and recommend rewards based on, for example, how well we leverage the work of others and contribute to the success of our colleagues. We monitor pay equity and career progress across multiple dimensions. Our total compensation opportunity is highly differentiated and is market competitive.
In order to manage our costs in a dynamic, competitive environment, in fiscal year 2023 we announced that base salaries of salaried employees would remain at fiscal year 2022 levels. Pay increases continue to be available for rewards-eligible hourly and equivalent employees. We will continue our practice of investing in stock for all rewards-eligible employees, salaried and hourly, and investing in bonuses for all eligible employees.
9
Item 1
Since 2016, we have reported on pay equity as part of our annual Diversity and Inclusion report. In 2022, we reported that all racial and ethnic minority employees in the U.S. combined earn $1.008 for every $1.000 earned by their white counterparts, that women in the U.S. earn $1.007 for every $1.000 earned by their counterparts who are men, and that women outside the U.S. earn $1.002 for every $1.000 earned by their counterparts outside the U.S. who are men. In this year's report, we again expanded our pay equity data beyond the U.S. to report on 61 additional countries (up from 12 last year), representing 99.8% of our global Microsoft workforce.
In addition, we began reporting on unadjusted median pay in our annual report, comparing total pay amounts for all employees regardless of factors such as job title, level, or tenure. For employees who are eligible for rewards, the analysis showed that total pay for women is 89.6% of total pay for men in the U.S. and 86.2% outside of the U.S., and total pay for racial and ethnic minorities in the U.S. is 89.9% of total pay for white employees. As we continue to increase representation for women and racial and ethnic minorities at more senior levels, and continue to ensure pay equity for all, the gap between the medians will reduce.
Our intended result is a global performance and development approach that fosters our culture, and competitive compensation that ensures equitable pay by role while supporting pay for performance.
Wellbeing and Hybrid Work
Microsoft is committed to supporting our employees' wellbeing while they are at work and in their personal lives. We have invested significantly in wellbeing, and offer a differentiated benefits package which includes many physical, emotional, and financial wellness programs including counseling through the Microsoft CARES Employee Assistance Program, mental wellbeing support, flexible fitness benefits, disability accommodations, savings and investment tools, adoption assistance, and back-up care for children and elders. Finally, our Occupational Health and Safety program helps ensure employees can stay safe while they are working.
We introduced Hybrid Workplace Flexibility Guidance to better support leaders, managers, and employees in hybrid work scenarios. Our ongoing survey data shows that 93% of employees value the flexibility related to work location, work site, and work hours, and 78% are satisfied with the quality of connection with co-workers. There is no one-size-fits-all approach to flexible work at Microsoft. As a company, we will continue to leverage data and research to inform decision making, balancing the needs of business, team, and individual.
Learning and Development
We offer a range of learning opportunities, including personalized opportunities on our internal and external learning portals, in-classroom learning, required learning on compliance and company culture, on-the-job advancement opportunities, and manager coaching. We also provide customized manager learning, new employee orientation, and tools for operating in a flexible hybrid work environment.
All Microsoft employees globally access our single Viva Learning tool for both required and personal choice learning. This includes courses focused on our core principles and compliance matters, such as Business Conduct, Privacy, Security Foundations, and Harassment Prevention. We also deliver skills training for employees based on their profession and role discipline.
We have over 27,000 people managers, all of whom must complete between 20-33 hours of compulsory training on leadership and management and are assigned additional targeted training on an ongoing basis related to people management, compliance, and culture.
10
Item 1
OPERATING SEGMENTS
We operate our business and report our financial performance using three segments: Productivity and Business Processes, Intelligent Cloud, and More Personal Computing. Our segments provide management with a comprehensive financial view of our key businesses. The segments enable the alignment of strategies and objectives across the development, sales, marketing, and services organizations, and they provide a framework for timely and rational allocation of resources within businesses.
Additional information on our operating segments and geographic and product information is contained in Note 19 – Segment Info
